content:
Derivative of

$y=\sqrt{\sin x+\sqrt{\sin x+\sqrt{\sin x+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \infty}}}$ is
explanation:
If $y=\sqrt{\mathrm{f}(x)+\sqrt{\mathrm{f}(x)+\sqrt{\mathrm{f}(x)+\ldots \infty}}}$, then

$$ \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{2 y-1} $$

Here, $\mathrm{f}(x)=\sin x \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\cos x$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\cos x}{2 y-1} $$

content: The approximate value of $\frac{1}{(2.002)^2}$ is
explanation:
We want to estimate the value of $\frac{1}{(2.002)^2}$.

Let’s use a simple approximation. Let $f(x)=\frac{1}{x^2}$.

The derivative of $f(x)$ is $f'(x) = -2x^{-3} = \frac{-2}{x^3}$.

Let $a=2$ and $h=0.002$, so $a+h=2.002$.

Using linear approximation: $f(a+h) \approx f(a) + h f'(a)$.

Substitute the values into the formula: $$ f(2.002) \approx f(2) + 0.002 \times f'(2) $$ We know $f(2) = \frac{1}{2^2} = \frac{1}{4}$ and $f'(2) = \frac{-2}{2^3} = \frac{-2}{8} = -\frac{1}{4}$.

So, $$ f(2.002) \approx \frac{1}{4} + 0.002 \times \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{0.002}{4} $$

This means $$ \frac{1}{(2.002)^2} \approx \frac{0.998}{4} \approx 0.2495 $$

content: If $x \cdot \log _e\left(\log _e x\right)-x^2+y^2=4(y>0)$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}$ at $x=\mathrm{e}$ is
explanation:
$$ x \log _e\left(\log _e x\right)-x^2+y^2=4 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \log _e\left(\log _e x\right)+x \cdot \frac{1}{\log _e x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}-2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x-\log _e\left(\log _e x\right)-\frac{1}{\log _e x}}{2 y} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{x=e}=\frac{2 e-\log _e\left(\log _e e\right)-\frac{1}{\log _e e}}{2 y} \\ & =\frac{2 e-1}{2 y} \end{aligned} $$

Putting $x=\mathrm{e}$ in (i), we get

e $\log _e\left(\log _e e\right)-e^2+y^2=4$

$\Rightarrow y^2=4+\mathrm{e}^{2]}$

$\Rightarrow y=\sqrt{4+\mathrm{e}^2}$

$$ \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=\mathrm{e}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{e}-1}{2 \sqrt{4+\mathrm{e}^2}} $$

content: If $u=\log (\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1})$ and $v=\sqrt{x+1}+\sqrt{x-1}$ then $\frac{d u}{d v}=\ldots$.
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{u}=\log (\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}}{2 \sqrt{x^2-1}}\right) \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x^2-1}} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{x+1}+\sqrt{x-1} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}} & =\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{x-1}+\sqrt{x+1}}{2 \sqrt{x^2-1}} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dv}} & =\frac{\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}}{\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{x-1}+\sqrt{x+1}}=\frac{-1}{\mathrm{v}} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=3 x^2+2 x \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)$, then $\mathrm{f}(x)=\ldots \ldots .$.
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}(x)=3 x^2+2 x \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=6 x+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=6(1)+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=-6 \\ & \text { and } \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)=6 \\ & \text { From (i) } \\ & \mathrm{f}(x)=3 x^2+2 x(-6)+6 \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ =3 x^2-12 x+6 \end{array} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\sqrt{1+\cos ^2\left(x^2\right)}$, then $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right)$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\sqrt{1+\cos ^2\left(x^2\right)} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1+\cos ^2\left(x^2\right)}} \cdot\left(2 \cos x^2\right) \cdot\left(-\sin x^2\right) \cdot(2 x) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-x \sin 2 x^2}{\sqrt{1+\cos ^2\left(x^2\right)}} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right)=\frac{-\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \sin \frac{2 \pi}{4}}{\sqrt{1+\cos ^2 \frac{\pi}{4}}}=-\frac{\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot 1}{\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}}=-\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{6}} \end{array} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\sin ^2 x}{1+\cot x}+\frac{\cos ^2 x}{1+\tan x}$, then the value of $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ is equal to
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}(x) & =\frac{\sin ^2 x}{1+\cot x}+\frac{\cos ^2 x}{1+\tan x} \\ & =\frac{\sin ^3 x}{\sin x+\cos x}+\frac{\cos ^3 x}{\sin x+\cos x}=\frac{\sin ^3 x+\cos ^3 x}{\sin x+\cos x} \\ & =\frac{(\sin x+\cos x)\left(\sin ^2 x-\sin x \cos x+\cos ^2 x\right)}{(\sin x+\cos x)} \\ & =\sin ^2 x-\sin x \cos x+\cos ^2 x \\ & =1-\sin x \cos x \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) & =1-\frac{\sin 2 x}{2} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=-\cos 2 x \\ \therefore & f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-\cos 2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{12 x-64 x^3}{1-48 x^2}\right)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{12 x-64 x^3}{1-48 x^2}\right) \\ & \text { Put } 4 x=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}(4 x) \\ \therefore \quad & y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 \tan \theta-\tan ^3 \theta}{1-3 \tan ^2 \theta}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}(\tan 3 \theta) \\ & =3 \theta \\ \therefore \quad & y=3 \tan ^{-1}(4 x) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3}{1+(4 x)^2} \times 4=\frac{12}{1+16 x^2} \end{array} $$

content:
If

$$ \sqrt{y-\sqrt{y-\sqrt{y-\ldots \ldots \ldots \infty}}}=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\ldots \ldots \ldots \infty}}} $$

then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$
explanation:
Let

$$ \sqrt{y-\sqrt{y-\sqrt{y-\ldots \ldots \ldots \infty}}}=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\ldots \ldots \ldots \infty}}}=\mathrm{p} $$

Consider,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \sqrt{y-\sqrt{y-\sqrt{y-\ldots \ldots \ldots \infty}}}=\mathrm{p} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{y-\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{p} \\ & \Rightarrow y-\mathrm{p}=\mathrm{p}^2 \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $y$, we get

$$ 1-\frac{d p}{d y}=2 p \frac{d p}{d y} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(2 p+1) \frac{d p}{d y}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d p}=2 p+1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Now, $\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\ldots \ldots \ldots \infty}}}=\mathrm{p}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sqrt{x+\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{p} \\ & \Rightarrow x+\mathrm{p}=\mathrm{p}^2 \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & 1+\frac{d p}{d x}=2 p \frac{d p}{d x} \\ & \Rightarrow(2 p-1) \frac{d p}{d x}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d p}=2 p-1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d p}}{\frac{d x}{d p}}=\frac{2 p+1}{2 p-1}\,\,\,\,\, ...[From (i) and (ii)]\end{aligned}\\ &\text { } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\frac{2\left(\frac{y-x}{2}\right)+1}{2\left(\frac{y-x}{2}\right)-1} \quad \cdots\left[\begin{array}{l} \text { since } x+\mathrm{p}=y-\mathrm{p} \\ \Rightarrow y-x=2 \mathrm{p} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=\frac{y-x}{2} \end{array}\right] \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\frac{y-x+1}{y-x-1} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow b^2 x^2+a^2 y^2=a^2 b^2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 b^2 x+2 a^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=-2 b^2 x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-b^2}{a^2}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{-b^2}{a^2}\left[\frac{y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^2}\right] \\ & =\frac{-b^2}{a^2 y^2}\left[y-x\left(\frac{-b^2 x}{a^2 y}\right)\right]\,\,\,\, \ldots[\text { From (ii)] }\\ & =\frac{-b^2}{a^2 y^2}\left[\frac{a^2 y^2+b^2 x^2}{a^2 y}\right] \\ & =\frac{-b^2}{a^2 y^2} \times \frac{a^2 b^2}{a^2 y} \,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & =\frac{-b^4}{a^2 y^3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=3 x^3+2 x^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+x \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime \prime}(3)$ then $\mathrm{f}(x)=$ __________ .
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{f}(x)=3 x^3+2 x^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+x \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime \prime}(3) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=9 x^2+4 x \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2) \quad \ldots \text { (i) } \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=18 x+4 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime \prime}(x)=18 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(3)=18 \end{array} $$

Substituting $x=1$ in equation (i), we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & f^{\prime}(1)=9+4 f^{\prime}(1)+f^{\prime \prime}(2) \\ \therefore & 3 f^{\prime}(1)+f^{\prime \prime}(2)=-9 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iv)\end{array} $$

Substituting $x=2$ in (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & f^{\prime \prime}(2)=36+4 f^{\prime}(1) \\ & \Rightarrow 7 f^{\prime}(1)=-45\,\,\,\,\, …[From (iv)]\end{aligned} $$

From (iii) and (iv), we get

$$ \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(1)=\frac{-45}{7}, f^{\prime \prime}(2)=\frac{72}{7} $$

Substituting values of $f^{\prime}(1), f^{\prime \prime}(2)$ and $f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3)$ in

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { given equation, we get } \\ & \begin{aligned} f(x) & =3 x^3+2 x^2\left(\frac{-45}{7}\right)+x\left(\frac{72}{7}\right)+18 \\ & =\frac{1}{7}\left(21 x^3-90 x^2+72 x+126\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $x=\sin \theta, y=\sin ^3 \theta$, then $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & x=\sin \theta \text { and } y=\sin ^3 \theta \\ \therefore & y=x^3 \\ \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=3 x^2 \\ \therefore & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=6 x \\ & \text { At } \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}, x=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}}=\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{x=\frac{1}{2}}=6\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=3 \end{array} $$

content: If $x=\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y-x^2}{x^2}\right)}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=1$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{e}^{\tan -1}\left(\frac{y-x^2}{x^2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \log x=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y-x^2}{x^2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \tan (\log x)=\frac{y-x^2}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow y=x^2 \tan (\log x)+x^2 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 x \tan (\log x)+x^2 \frac{\sec ^2(\log x)}{x}+2 x \\ & \quad=x \sec ^2(\log x)+2 x \tan (\log x)+2 x \\ & \left.\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dx}}\right|_{x=1}=1+2=3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{4 \sin 2 x}{\cos 2 x-6 \sin ^2 x}\right]$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=0$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} y & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 \sin 2 x}{\cos 2 x-6 \sin ^2 x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4(2 \sin x \cos x)}{\left(\cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x\right)-6 \sin ^2 x}\right] \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{8 \sin x \cos x}{\cos ^2 x-7 \sin ^2 x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{8 \tan x}{1-7 \tan 2 x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{7 \tan x+\tan x}{1-7 \tan x \cdot \tan x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}(7 \tan x)+\tan -1(\tan x) \\ \therefore \quad y & =\tan ^{-1}(7 \tan x)+x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+(7 \tan x)^2} \cdot 7 \sec ^2 x+1 \\ & \quad\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\frac{7 \sec ^2 x}{1+49 \tan ^2 x}+1 \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{x=0}=\frac{7 \sec ^2 0}{1+49 \tan ^2 0}+1=\frac{7}{1+0}+1=8 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $x=a \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t), y=b \cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t)$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \quad x & =a \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t) \\ \quad \therefore \quad \frac{d x}{d t} & =a \sin 2 t(-2 \sin 2 t)+(1+\cos 2 t)(2 a \cos 2 t) \\ & =-2 a \sin ^2 2 t+2 a \cos 2 t+2 a \cos ^2 2 t \\ & =2 a \cos 2 t+2 a\left(\cos ^2 2 t-\sin ^2 2 t\right) \\ & =2 a \cos 2 t+2 a \cos 4 t \\ & =2 a(\cos 2 t+\cos 4 t) \\ y & =b \cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d t} & =b \cos 2 t(2 \sin 2 t)+(1-\cos 2 t)(-2 b \sin 2 t) \\ & =b(2 \sin 2 t \cos 2 t)-2 b \sin 2 t+b(2 \sin 2 t \cos 2 t) \\ & =b \sin 4 t-2 b \sin 2 t+b \sin 4 t \\ & =2 b(\sin 4 t-\sin 2 t) \\ \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{2 b(\sin 4 t-\sin 2 t)}{2 a(\cos 2 t+\cos 4 t)} \\ & =\frac{b}{a} \frac{(2 \cos 3 t \sin t)}{(2 \cos 3 t \cos t)} \\ & =\frac{b}{a} \tan t \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{y+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{y+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots . \infty}}}}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & y=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{y+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{y+\ldots \infty}}}} \\ & \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{y+y}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(y^2-x\right)=\sqrt{2 y} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(y^2-x\right)^2=2 y \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating both sides w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & 2\left(y^2-x\right)\left(2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-1\right)=2 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y^2-x}{2 y^3-2 x y-1} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(1)=3, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=2$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left\{\log \left[\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{e}^x+2 x\right)\right]\right\}$ at $x=0$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{d}{d x}\left\{\log \left[f\left(e^x+2 x\right)\right]\right\} \\ & =\frac{1}{f\left(e^x+2 x\right)} f^{\prime}\left(e^x+2 x\right) \cdot\left(e^x+2\right) \\ & \frac{d}{d x}\left\{\log \left[f\left(e^x+2 x\right)\right]\right\}_{x=0} \\ & =\frac{1}{f(1)} \cdot f^{\prime}(1) \cdot(1+2) \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \cdot 2 \cdot 3=2 \end{aligned} $$

content: Derivative of $x^{\left(x^x\right)}$ is
explanation:
Let $x^x=\mathrm{t}$

$\therefore \quad \log \mathrm{t}=x \log x$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1+\log x \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dx}} & =\mathrm{t}(1+\log x) \\ & =x^x(1+\log x) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Let $y=x^{x^x}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \log y & =\log x^{x^x} \\ & =x^x \log x \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =x^x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{~d} x} x^x \\ & =x^{x-1}+x^x \log x(1+\log x) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =x^{x^x}\left[x^{x-1}+x^x \log x(1+\log x)\right] \end{aligned} $$

content: For $N \in \mathbb{N}, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{d} x^{\mathrm{n}}}(\log x)=$
explanation:
Let $y=\log x$

Differentiate w.r.t. $x$

$$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \log x=\frac{1}{x} $$

Again, differentiating w.r.t. $x$

$$ \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{-1}{x^2} $$

By continuity this process, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d}^3 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^3}=(-1)^2\left(\frac{2}{x^3}\right) \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d}^4 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^4}=\frac{(-1)^3 3!}{x^4}=\frac{-6}{x^4} \end{aligned} $$

We observe that,

$$ \frac{\mathrm{d}^{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{~d} x^{\mathrm{n}}}(\log x)=\frac{(-1)^{\mathrm{n}-1} \cdot(\mathrm{n}-1)!}{x^{\mathrm{n}}} $$

content:
If $x=\operatorname{acos}^3 \theta y=\operatorname{asin}^3 \theta$

Then $\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & x=a \cos ^3 \theta \\ & \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a \times 3 \cos ^2 \theta \times(-\sin \theta) \\ & =-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \cdot \sin \theta \\ & y=a \sin ^3 \theta \\ & \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \times 3 \sin ^2 \theta \cdot \cos \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta}{-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta}=-\tan \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}=\sqrt{1+(\tan \theta)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{1+\tan ^2 \theta}=\sec \theta \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\sin ^2\left(\cot ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right)\right)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$
explanation:
Let $y=\sin ^2\left\{\cot ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right)\right\}$

Put $x=\cos \theta$

$$ \therefore \quad y=\sin ^2\left\{\cot ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-\cos \theta}{1+\cos \theta}}\right)\right\} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad=\sin ^2\left\{\cot ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}}\right)\right\} \\ & \quad=\sin ^2\left\{\cot ^{-1}\left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right\} \\ & \quad=\sin ^2\left\{\cot ^{-1}\left(\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right)\right\} \\ & \quad=\sin ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad y=\cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1+\cos \theta}{2}=\frac{1+x}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\mathrm{a}^x \cdot \mathrm{~b}^{2 x-1}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ y=a^x b^{2 x-1} $$

Taking log on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \log y=\log \left(a^x \cdot b^{2 x-1}\right)=\log a^x+\log b^{2 x-1} \\ & \log y=x \log a+(2 x-1) \log b \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t ' $x$ ' we get

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x} & =\log a+(2) \log b \\ & =\log a+\log b^2 \\ \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x} & =\log \left(a \cdot b^2\right) \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =y \log \left(a \cdot b^2\right) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Again differencing w.r.t. ' $x$ ' we got

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \log \left(\mathrm{ab}^2\right) \\ & =\left[y \log \left(\mathrm{a} \cdot \mathrm{~b}^2\right)\right]\left[\log \left(\mathrm{a} \cdot \mathrm{~b}^2\right)\right] \,\,\,\,\,\text { ...[From (ii)] }\\ & \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=y\left[\log \left(\mathrm{ab}^2\right)\right]^2 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x) \mathrm{e}^{\frac{y}{x}}=x$, then $x^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x) \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\frac{y}{x}}=x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{y}{x}}=\frac{x}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=\log \left(\frac{x}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=\log x-\log (\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y \cdot 1}{x^2}=\frac{1}{x}-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x} \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y=x^2\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y=\frac{\mathrm{a} x}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Again, differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x) \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{a} x(\mathrm{~b})}{(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow x^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{x^2 \mathrm{a}^2}{(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow x^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{a} x}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow x^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\left(x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y\right)^2 \ldots[\text { From (i) }]\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\frac{\mathrm{K}^{\cos ^{-1} x}}{1+\mathrm{K}^{\cos ^{-1} x}}$ and $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{K}^{\cos ^{-1} x}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=$
explanation:
Given:

$$y = \frac{K^{\cos^{-1} x}}{1 + K^{\cos^{-1} x}}$$

Let

$$t = K^{\cos^{-1} x}$$

So,

$$y = \frac{t}{1+t}$$

Let us find $\frac{dy}{dt}$:

Step 1: Differentiate $y$ with respect to $t$:

$ y = \frac{t}{1+t} $

$ \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{(1 + t) \cdot 1 - t \cdot 1}{(1+t)^2} $

$ \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{1 + t - t}{(1+t)^2} $

$ \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{1}{(1+t)^2} $

Step 2: Substitute $t = K^{\cos^{-1} x}$:

$ \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{1}{\big(1 + K^{\cos^{-1} x}\big)^2} $

Correct answer is:

Option C:

$ \boxed{\frac{1}{\left(1+\mathrm{K}^{\cos ^{-1} x}\right)^2}} $

content: If $\mathrm{a}\left(4+x^2\right)=x$ and $y-x^3=\mathrm{a}^2$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=1$ is $\qquad$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } \mathrm{a}\left(4+x^2\right)=x \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{a}=\frac{x}{4+x^2} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\left(4+x^2\right)-x(0+2 x)}{\left(4+x^2\right)^2} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{4-x^2}{\left(4+x^2\right)^2} \\ & y-x^3=\mathrm{a}^2 \end{array}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-3 x^2=2 \mathrm{a} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-3 x^2=2\left(\frac{x}{4+x^2}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{4-x^2}{\left(4+x^2\right)^2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2 x\left(4-x^2\right)}{\left(4+x^2\right)^3}+3 x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(x=1)}=\frac{2 \times 3}{(5)^3}+3=\frac{381}{125} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $x^y+y^x=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{b}}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=1, y=2$ is
explanation:
Given, $x^y+y^x=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{b}}$

Let $\mathrm{u}=x^y$ and $\mathrm{v}=y^x$

Then, $\mathrm{u}+\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{b}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

Consider, $\mathrm{u}=x^y$

Taking $\log$ on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \log \mathrm{u}=\log x^y \\ & \Rightarrow \log \mathrm{u}=y \log x \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\log x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\frac{y}{x}+\log x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^y\left[\frac{y}{x}+\log x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right] \\ & \mathrm{v}=y^x \end{aligned} $$

Taking $\log$ on both sides, we get $\log \mathrm{v}=x \log y$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x\left(\frac{1}{y}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\log y(1) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}\left[\frac{x}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\log y\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y^x\left[\frac{x}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\log y\right] \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating (i) w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^y\left[\frac{y}{x}+\log x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right]+y^x\left[\frac{x}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\log y\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2+0+2\left(\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(1,2)}+\log 2\right)=0 [\operatorname{At} x=1, y=2]\end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(1,2)}=-2(1+\log 2) $$

content: If $u=\frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{\tan ^{-1} x+1}$ and $v=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$ then $\frac{d u}{d v}=$
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{u}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{\tan ^{-1} x+1}$ and $\mathrm{v}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$

Put $\mathrm{f}(x)=\tan ^{-1} x \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^2}$

$$ \therefore \quad u=\frac{f(x)}{f(x)+1} \text { and } v=\tan ^{-1}(f(x)) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{d u}{d x} & =\frac{(f(x)+1) \cdot f^{\prime}(x)-f(x) \cdot\left(f^{\prime}(x)+0\right)}{(f(x)+1)^2} \\ & =\frac{f(x) \cdot f^{\prime}(x)+f^{\prime}(x)-f(x) \cdot f^{\prime}(x)}{(f(x)+1)^2} \\ & =\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{(f(x)+1)^2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\frac{1}{1+x^2}}{\left(\tan ^{-1} x+1\right)^2}=\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)\left(\tan ^{-1} x+1\right)^2} \\ & \text { Now, } \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{1+[\mathrm{f}(x)]^2} \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \\ & =\frac{1}{1+\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2} \times \frac{1}{1+x^2} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dv}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}}{\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}}=\frac{\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)\left(\tan ^{-1} x+1\right)^2}}{\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)\left(1+\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2\right)}}=\frac{1+\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2}{\left(\tan ^{-1} x+1\right)^2} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{y}=x^x+x^{\frac{1}{x}}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $y_1=x^x$

$$ \therefore \quad \log y_1=x \log x $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \frac{1}{y_1} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y_1}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1+\log x \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y_1}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^x(1+\log x) \end{array} $$

Let $y_2=x^{\frac{1}{x}}$

$$ \therefore \quad \log y_2=\frac{1}{x} \log x $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \frac{1}{y_2} \frac{d y_2}{d x}=\frac{1}{x^2}-\frac{\log x}{x^2} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d y_2}{d x}=x^{\frac{1}{x}} \frac{1}{x^2}(1-\log x) \\ & y^{\frac{1}{x}} \\ \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y_1}{d x}+\frac{d y_2}{d x}=x^x(1+\log x)+x^{\frac{1}{x}} \frac{1}{x^2}(1-\log x) \end{array} $$

content: If $f(1)=1, f^{\prime}(1)=3$, then the derivative of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2$ at $x=1$ is
explanation:
Let $y=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(f(f(x))) \cdot f^{\prime}(f(x)) \cdot f^{\prime}(x)+2 f(x) \cdot f^{\prime}(x) \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{x=1}=f^{\prime}(f(f(1))) f^{\prime}(f(1)) \cdot f^{\prime}(1)+2 f(1) \cdot f^{\prime}(1) \\ & =f^{\prime}(f(1)) \cdot f^{\prime}(1) \cdot f^{\prime}(1)+2 f(1) \cdot f^{\prime}(1) \\ & =3(3)(3)+2(1)(3) \\ & =27+6 \\ & =33 \end{array} $$

content: If $y=\log _{\mathrm{e}} x^3+3 \sin ^{-1} x+\mathrm{kx}^2$ and $y^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=2 \sqrt{3}$, then $k=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} y & =\log x^3+3 \sin ^{-1} x+\mathrm{k} x^2 \\ & =3 \log x+3 \sin ^{-1} x+\mathrm{k} x^2 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y^{\prime}=\frac{3}{x}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}+2 \mathrm{k} x \\ \therefore & y^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{k}}{2} \\ \therefore & 2 \sqrt{3}=6+\frac{6}{\sqrt{3}}+\mathrm{k} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{k}=2 \sqrt{3}-6-\frac{6}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & =\frac{6-6 \sqrt{3}-6}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & =-6 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content:
The derivative of

$$ y=(1-x)(2-x) \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots(\mathrm{n}-x) $$

at $x=1$ is
explanation:
$$ y=(1-x)(2-x)(3-x)\ldots(n-x) $$

Let's write $y$ as $y = (1-x) \cdot f(x)$,

where $f(x) = (2-x)(3-x) \ldots (n-x)$

We are going to use the product rule to find the derivative of $y$ with respect to $x$:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} = (1-x) \cdot f'(x) + f(x) \cdot (-1) $$

Now, we find the value at $x = 1$:

At $x=1$, $f(x) = (2-1)(3-1)\ldots(n-1) = (n-1)!$

Also, $(1-x)$ becomes $0$ when $x=1$, so $(1-x) \cdot f'(x) = 0$

So, $$ \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{x=1} = 0 + (n-1)! \cdot (-1) = - (n-1)! $$

content: The first derivative of the function $\left(\cos ^{-1}\left(\sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right)+x^x\right)$ with respect to $x$ at $x=1$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\left(\cos ^{-1}\left(\sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right)+x^x\right) \\ & \text { Put } \mathrm{u}=\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}} \\ & \quad y=\cos ^{-1}(\sin \mathrm{u})+x^x \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \mathrm{u}}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}(\sin \mathrm{u})}{\mathrm{d} x}+x^x[1+\log x] \\ & \quad=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}+x^x[1+\log x] \left(\because \mathrm{u}=\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}}} \times \cos \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}} \\ & \quad=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}} \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+x^x[1+\log x] \\ & \quad=\frac{-\sqrt{2}}{4 \sqrt{1+x}}+x^x[1+\log x] \\ & \therefore \quad\left[\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right]_{x=1}=\frac{-\sqrt{2}}{4 \sqrt{2}}+1 .[1+\log 1] \\ & \quad=\frac{-1}{4}+1 \\ & \quad=\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $x^{\frac{2}{5}}+y^{\frac{2}{5}}=\mathrm{a}^{\frac{2}{5}}$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$
explanation:
$$ x^{\frac{2}{5}}+y^{\frac{2}{5}}=\mathrm{a}^{\frac{2}{5}} $$

Differentiating w.r.t $x$, we get

$$ \frac{2}{5} x^{\frac{2}{5}-1}+\frac{2}{5} y^{\frac{2}{5}-1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} y^{\frac{-3}{5}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-2}{5} x^{\frac{-3}{5}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\frac{x^{\frac{-3}{5}}}{y^{\frac{-3}{5}}} \\ & =-\frac{y^{\frac{3}{5}}}{x^{\frac{3}{5}}} \\ & =-\sqrt[5]{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^3} \end{aligned} $$

content: For $\mathrm{n} \in \mathbb{N}$ if $y=\mathrm{a} x^{\mathrm{n}+1}+\mathrm{b} x^{-\mathrm{n}}$, then $x^2 \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &y=a x^{n+1}+b x^{-n}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=a(n+1) x^{\mathrm{n}+1-1}+b(-n) x^{-\mathrm{n}-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=a(n+1) x^{\mathrm{n}}-b n x^{-\mathrm{n}-1} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Again differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2} & =a(n+1) n x^{n-1}-b n(-n-1) x^{-n-1-1} \\ & =a(n+1) n x^{n-1}+b n(n+1) x^{-n-2} \\ & =n(n+1) \frac{a x^n}{x}+n(n+1) b \frac{x^{-n}}{x^2} \\ \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2} & =\frac{n(n+1)}{x^2}\left[a x^{n+1}+b x^{-n}\right] \\ \therefore \quad x^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2} & =n(n+1) y \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The derivative of $\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}-1\right)$ is
explanation:
Let $y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}-1\right)$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =\frac{1}{1+\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}-1\right)^2} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}-1\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{1+2+x^2-2 \sqrt{1+x^2}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1+x^2}} \times 2 x \\ & =\frac{x}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}\right)\left(x^2-2 \sqrt{1+x^2}+3\right)} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{g}(x)=[\mathrm{f}(2 \mathrm{f}(x)+2)]^2$ and $\mathrm{f}(0)=-1, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)=1$ then $g^{\prime}(0)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{g}(x)=\{\mathrm{f}[2 \mathrm{f}(x)+2]\}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{~g}^{\prime}(x)=2 \mathrm{f}[2 \mathrm{f}(x)+2] \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}[2 \mathrm{f}(x)+2] \cdot 2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \\ & \therefore \quad g^{\prime}(0)=2 f[2 f(0)+2] \cdot f^{\prime}[2 f(0)+2] \cdot 2 f^{\prime}(0) \\ & =2 \mathrm{f}[2(-1)+2] \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(2(-1)+2) \cdot 2(1) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(0)=4 \mathrm{f}(0) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0) \\ & =4(-1)(1) \\ & =-4 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{a \sin x+b \cos x}{c \sin x+d \cos x}$ is decreasing for all $x$ then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &f(x)=\frac{a \sin x+b \cos x}{c \sin x+d \cos x}\\ &\mathrm{f}(x) \text { will be decreasing, if } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)<0 \end{aligned}$$

$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{1}{(c \sin x+d \cos x)^2}[(c \sin x+d \cos x)(a \cos x-b \sin x) \\ -(a \sin x+b \cos x)(c \cos x-d \sin x)]<0\end{aligned}$

$\begin{array}{r}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ac} \sin x \cos x-\mathrm{bc} \sin ^2 x+\mathrm{ad} \cos ^2 x \\ -\mathrm{bd} \sin x \cos x-\mathrm{acsin} x \cos x+\mathrm{ad} \sin ^2 x \\ -\mathrm{bc} \cos ^2 x+\mathrm{bd} \sin x \cos x<0\end{array}$

content: If $y=\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=1$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} y & =\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \tan \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1+x^2} \\ & =\sqrt{1+x^2} \cdot \frac{x}{1+x^2} \end{aligned}$$

$\ldots\left[\because \tan ^{-1} x=\sec ^{-1} \sqrt{1+x^2}\right]$

content: If $f(1)=1, f^{\prime}(1)=5$, then the derivative of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2$ at $x=1$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let } y=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2\\ &\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(x)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)+2 \mathrm{f}(x) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=1}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(1))) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(1)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+2 f(1) \cdot f^{\prime}(1)$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(1)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \cdot 5+2 \cdot 1 \cdot 5 \\ & =\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \cdot 5 \cdot 5+2 \cdot 1 \cdot 5 \\ & =5 \cdot 5 \cdot 5+2 \cdot 1 \cdot 5 \\ & =135 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x=\sin \theta, y=\sin ^3 \theta$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & x=\sin \theta \text { and } y=\sin ^3 \theta \\ & \therefore \quad y=x^3 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=3 x^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=6 x \\ & \text { At } \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}, x=\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=1 \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}}=\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{x=1}=6(1)=6 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & y=\frac{x^{\frac{2}{3}}-x^{-\frac{1}{3}}}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}+x^{-\frac{1}{3}}} \\ &=\frac{x^{-\frac{1}{3}}(x-1)}{x^{-\frac{1}{3}}(x+1)} \\ & \therefore \quad y=\frac{x-1}{x+1} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{(x+1) \cdot 1-(x-1) \cdot 1}{(x+1)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2}{(x+1)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1)^2 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The derivative of $\sin ^{-1}\left(2 x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right)$ w.r.t. $\sin ^{-1}\left(3 x-4 x^3\right)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } y=\sin ^{-1}\left(2 x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right) \\ & \text { and } z=\sin ^{-1}\left(3 x-4 x^3\right) \\ & \text { Put } x=\sin \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\sin ^{-1} x \\ \therefore \quad & y=\sin ^{-1}\left(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \theta}\right) \text { and } \\ & \mathrm{z}=\sin ^{-1}\left(3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^3 \theta\right) \\ \Rightarrow & y=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta) \text { and } \mathrm{z}=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 3 \theta) \\ \Rightarrow y & =2 \theta=2 \sin ^{-1} x \text { and } \mathrm{z}=3 \theta=3 \sin ^{-1} x \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dz}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} z}{\mathrm{~d} x}}=\frac{2}{3} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $f(1)=1, f^{\prime}(1)=3$, then the derivative of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2$ at $x=1$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } y & =\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2 \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}= & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(x)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)+2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \\ \left.\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=1} & =\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(1))) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(1)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+2 \mathrm{f}(1) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \\ & =3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3+2 \cdot 1 \cdot 3 \\ & =33 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$ y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots \ldots \ldots(n x+1)]^2 $$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=0$ is
explanation:
$$y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots(n x+1)]^2$$

Taking ' log' on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \log y=2[\log (x+1)+\log (2 x+1)+\log (3 x+1) \\ & +\ldots+\log (n x+1)] \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2\left(\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{2 x+1}+\frac{3}{3 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n} x+1}\right) \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 y\left(\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{2 x+1}+\frac{3}{3 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n} x+1}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Now at $x=0,y=$ $$[\underbrace{(1)(1)(1) \ldots(1)}_{n \text { times }}]^2$$ $=1$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=0} & =2(1)\left(\frac{1}{0+1}+\frac{2}{0+1}+\frac{3}{0+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{0+1}\right) \\ & =2(1+2+3+\ldots+\mathrm{n}) \\ & =2 \times \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}=\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $(a+\sqrt{2} b \cos x)(a-\sqrt{2} b \cos y)=a^2-b^2$, where $\mathrm{a}>\mathrm{b}>0$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}$ at $\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &(a+\sqrt{2} b \cos x)(a-\sqrt{2} b \cos y)=a^2-b^2\\\\ &\text { Differentiating both sides w.r.t. } y \text {, we get }\\\\ &\begin{aligned} & (a+\sqrt{2} b \cos x)(\sqrt{2} b \sin y) \\\\ & +(\mathrm{a}-\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~b} \cos y)\left(-\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~b} \sin x \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}\right)=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~b} \sin y(\mathrm{a}+\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~b} \cos x)}{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~b} \sin x(\mathrm{a}-\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~b} \cos y)} \\\\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}\right)_{\left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})}{\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b})}=\frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x=2 \cos \theta-\cos 2 \theta$ and $y=2 \sin \theta-\sin 2 \theta$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{d x^2}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & x=2 \cos \theta-\cos 2 \theta \text { and } y=2 \sin \theta-\sin 2 \theta \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=-2 \sin \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta \\ & \text { and } \frac{d y}{d \theta}=2 \cos \theta-2 \cos 2 \theta \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}=\frac{2 \cos \theta-2 \cos 2 \theta}{-2 \sin \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta} \\ & =\frac{\cos \theta-\cos 2 \theta}{\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta} \\ & =\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{3 \theta}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)}{2 \cos \left(\frac{3 \theta}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)}=\tan \left(\frac{3 \theta}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}\left(\tan \frac{3 \theta}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & =\sec ^2\left(\frac{3 \theta}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sin 2 \theta-2 \sin \theta} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$=\frac{3}{4}\left[\frac{\sec ^2\left(\frac{3 \theta}{2}\right)}{\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta}\right]$

content: If $y=(\sin x)^{\tan x}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation:

content: If $y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\log x^2}{1+(\log x)^2}\right)$, then $\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\mathrm{at ~} x=1}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\log x^2}{1+(\log x)^2}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \log x}{1+(\log x)^2}\right) \\ & \text { Let } \log x=\tan \theta \\ \therefore \quad & y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1+\tan 2 \theta}\right)=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta)=2 \theta \\ \therefore \quad & y=2 \tan ^{-1}(\log x) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d x}=2 \times \frac{1}{1+(\log x)^2} \times \frac{1}{x} \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{d x}\right)\mid_{a t ~x=1}=2 \end{array}$$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=(1+x)\left(1+x^2\right)\left(1+x^4\right)\left(1+x^8\right)$, then $f^{\prime}(1)=$

content: If $x^2+y^2=\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}, x^4+y^4=\mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & x^2+y^2=\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \text { and } x^4+y^4=\mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2} \\ \therefore \quad & \left(x^2+y^2\right)^2=\left(\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right)^2 \\ \therefore \quad & x^4+y^4+2 x^2 y^2=\mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2}+2 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2}+2 x^2 y^2=\mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2}+2 \\ \therefore \quad & x^2 y^2=1 \\ \therefore \quad & y^2=\frac{1}{x^2} \end{array}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-2}{x^3} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-1}{x^3 y} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y$ is a function of $x$ and $\log (x+y)=2 x y$, then the value of $y^{\prime}(0)$ is
explanation:

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{1}{x+y}\right)\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=2\left(x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1-2 x y-2 y^2}{2 x^2+2 x y-1} \end{aligned}$$

Putting $x=0$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & y=1 \\ \therefore \quad & y^{\prime}(0)=\frac{1-0-2}{0+0-1}=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x^2 y^2=\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=1$ and $y=2$ is
explanation:
To find $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x = 1$ and $y = 2$, start by differentiating the given equation $x^2 y^2 = \sin^{-1} x + \cos^{-1} x$ with respect to $x$.

First, note the identity:

$ \sin^{-1} x + \cos^{-1} x = \frac{\pi}{2} $

Thus, the equation simplifies to:

$ x^2 y^2 = \frac{\pi}{2} $

Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$:

$ \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d}x} (x^2 y^2) = 0 $

Apply the product rule to $x^2 y^2$:

$ 2x y^2 + x^2 \cdot 2y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} = 0 $

Simplify:

$ 2x y^2 + 2x^2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} = 0 $

Isolate $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$:

$ 2x^2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} = -2x y^2 $

$ x^2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} = -x y^2 $

Divide both sides by $x^2 y$:

$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} = -\frac{x y^2}{x^2 y} = -\frac{y}{x} $

Evaluate at $x = 1$ and $y = 2$:

$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \bigg|_{x=1, y=2} = -\frac{2}{1} = -2 $

Thus, the correct answer is $\boxed{-2}$.

content: If $$y = {{\sin x} \over {1 + {{\cos x} \over {1 + {{\sin x} \over {1 + {{\cos x} \over {...}}}}}}}}$$, then $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is given by

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y(-\sin x)+\cos x & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \sin x+(1+y) \cos x \end{aligned}$$

content: The curve $x^4-2 x y^2+y^2+3 x-3 y=0$ cuts the X -axis at $(0,0)$ at an angle of
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given equation of curve is }\\ &x^4-2 x y^2+y^2+3 x-3 y=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Slope of tangent $=m=\frac{d y}{d x}$ at $(0,0)$

Differentiating (i) w.r.to $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 4 x^3-2 x \cdot 2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y^2(2)+2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+3-3 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{4 x^3-2 y^2+3}{4 x y-2 y+3} \\ \therefore\quad& \mathrm{~m}=\left.\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{(0,0)}=\frac{4(0)-2(0)+3}{4(0) \cdot(0)-2(0)+3}=\frac{3}{3}=1 \\ \therefore\quad& \mathrm{~m}=1 \\ \therefore\quad& \tan \theta=1 \\ \therefore\quad& \theta=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y$ is a function of $x$ and $\log (x+y)=2 x y$, then the value of $y^{\prime}(0)$ is

content: If $y=a x^{n+1}+b x^{-n}$, then $x^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=$
explanation:
$$y=a x^{\mathrm{n}+1}+b x^{-\mathrm{n}}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=a(n+1) x^{n+1-1}+b(-n) x^{-n-1} \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=a(n+1) x^n-b n x^{-n-1} \end{aligned}$$

Again differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{dx}} & =\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{n}+1)^{\mathrm{n}} x^{\mathrm{n}-1}-\mathrm{bn}(-\mathrm{n}-1) x^{-\mathrm{n}-1-1} \\ & =\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{n}+1) \mathrm{n} x^{\mathrm{n}-1}+\mathrm{bn}(\mathrm{n}+1) x^{-\mathrm{n}-2} \\ & =\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1) \frac{\mathrm{a} x^{\mathrm{n}}}{x}+n(\mathrm{n}+1) b \frac{x^{-\mathrm{n}}}{x^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{n(n+1)}{x^2}\left[a x^{n+1}+b x^{-n}\right] \\ \therefore \quad & x^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=n(n+1) y \end{array}$$

content: If $y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 x}{2}-\frac{x^3}{2}\right)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 x}{2}-\frac{x^3}{2}\right) \\ &=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 x}{2}-4\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)^3\right) \\ & \text { Put } \frac{x}{2}=\sin \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad y=\sin ^{-1}\left(3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^3 \theta\right) \\ &=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 3 \theta) \\ &=3 \theta \\ & \therefore \quad y=3 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=3 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{4-x^2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\log (x+y)=\sin (x+y)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \log (x+y)=\sin (x+y) \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=\cos (x+y)\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x+y}+\frac{1}{x+y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\cos (x+y)+\cos (x+y) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(\frac{1}{x+y}-\cos (x+y)\right)=\cos (x+y)-\frac{1}{x+y} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^x, \mathrm{~g}(x)=\sin ^{-1} x$ and $\mathrm{h}(x)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))$, then $\left(\frac{h^{\prime}(x)}{h(x)}\right)^2$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}(x) & =\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~g}(x)) \\ & =\mathrm{f}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right) \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{h}(x) & =\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}^{\prime}(x) & =\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\frac{\mathrm{h}^{\prime}(x)}{\mathrm{h}(x)}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}}{\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$

$$\therefore \quad\left(\frac{h^{\prime}(x)}{h(x)}\right)^2=\frac{1}{1-x^2}$$

content: If for $x \in\left(0, \frac{1}{4}\right)$, the derivative of $\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{6 x \sqrt{x}}{1-9 x^3}\right)$ is $\sqrt{x} \cdot g(x)$, then $g(x)$ equals

Let $y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{6 x \sqrt{x}}{1-9 x^3}\right)$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 x \sqrt{x}+3 x \sqrt{x}}{1-3 x \sqrt{x} \cdot 3 x \sqrt{x}}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}(3 x \sqrt{x})+\tan ^{-1} 3 x \sqrt{x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &y=2 \tan ^{-1}(3 x \cdot \sqrt{x})\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{2}{1+(3 x \sqrt{x})^2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(3 x \cdot \sqrt{x}) \\ & =\frac{2}{1+9 x^3} \cdot \frac{9}{2} \sqrt{x} \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{9 \sqrt{x}}{1+9 x^3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { Comparing with } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\sqrt{x} \cdot \mathrm{~g}(x)\\ &\therefore \quad g(x)=\frac{9}{1+9 x^3} \end{aligned}$$

content: Derivative of $\mathrm{e}^x$ w.r.t. $\sqrt{x}$ is
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{e}^x$ and $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{x}$

Differentiating u and v w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d u}{d x}=e^x, \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\frac{e^x}{\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}}=2 e^x \sqrt{x} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x^2-x}{x^2+2 x}$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}\left(\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)\right)$ at $x=2$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=y=\frac{x^2-x}{x^2+2 x}=\frac{x(x-1)}{x(x+2)} \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{x-1}{x+2} \\ & \Rightarrow y x+2 y=x-1 \quad \Rightarrow \frac{2 y+1}{1-y}=x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)=\frac{2 x+1}{1-x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{2 x+1}{1-x}\right) \\ &=\frac{(1-x)(2)-(2 x+1)(-1)}{(1-x)^2} \\ &=\frac{2-2 x+2 x+1}{(1-x)^2}=\frac{3}{(1+x)^2} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{f}^{-1}(x) \text { at } x=2=\frac{3}{(1-2)^2}=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=A \cos \mathrm{n} x+\mathrm{B} \sin \mathrm{nx}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=$
explanation:
$$y=A \cos n x+B \sin n x\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Differentiating w.r.to $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=-A n(\sin n x)+B n \cos n x \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=-n A \sin n x+n B \cos n x \end{aligned}$$

Again differentiating w.r.to $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2} & =-\mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~A} \cos \mathrm{n} x-\mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~B} \sin \mathrm{n} x \\ & =-\mathrm{n}^2[\mathrm{~A} \cos \mathrm{n} x+\mathrm{B} \sin \mathrm{n} x] \\ \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2} & =-\mathrm{n}^2 y\quad\text{... [from (i)]} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}}$ and $\mathrm{g}(x)=\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)$ then the value of $g^{\prime}(1)$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=x^3+\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2+\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Given that $\mathrm{g}(x)=\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad \operatorname{gof}(x)=x \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{f}(x))=x \end{array}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get $\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(x)) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=1$

for $x=0$, we get

$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(0)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)=1$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(1)=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)}=\frac{1}{0+\frac{1}{2}}=2$$

content: If $y=\left((x+1)(4 x+1)(9 x+1) \ldots\left(\mathrm{n}^2 x+1\right)\right)^2$, then $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}$ at $x=0$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & y=\left((x+1)(4 x+1)(9 x+1) \ldots\left(\mathrm{n}^2 x+1\right)\right)^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \log y=2[\log (x+1)+\log (4 x+1)+\log (9 x+1) \left.+\ldots+\log \left(\mathrm{n}^2 x+1\right)\right] \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} \quad \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =2\left[\frac{1}{(x+1)}+\frac{4}{4 x+1}+\frac{9}{9 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{n}^2 x+1}\right] \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =2 y\left[\frac{(1)^2}{x+1}+\frac{(2)^2}{4 x+1}+\frac{(3)^2}{9 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{n}^2 x+1}\right] \\ \left.\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=0} & =2 y(0)\left[1^2+2^2+3^2+\ldots+\mathrm{n}^2\right] \\ & =2(1) \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots \ldots \ldots(n x+1)]^4$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=0$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots(\mathrm{n} x+1)]^4 \\ & \Rightarrow \log y=4[\log (x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots(\mathrm{n} x+1)] \\ & \Rightarrow \log y=4[\log (x+1)+\log (2 x+1) \\ & \quad+\log (3 x+1)+\ldots+\log (\mathrm{n} x+1)] \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=4\left[\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{2 x+1}+\frac{3}{3 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n} x+1}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=0}=4(1+2+3+\ldots \mathrm{n}) \quad \ldots[\text { At } x=0, y=1]\\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=0}=4\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}\right)=2 \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1) \end{aligned}$$

content: Derivative of $\sin ^2 x$ with respect to $e^{\cos x}$
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{u}=\sin ^2 x, \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{e}^{\cos x}$

$$\mathrm{u}=\sin ^2 x$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{d u}{d x}=2 \sin x \cdot \cos x$$

Consider, $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{e}^{\cos x}$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{d v}{d x} & =-e^{\cos x} \cdot \sin x \\ \frac{d u}{d v} & =\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}} =\frac{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x}{-e^{\cos x} \cdot \sin x}\\ &=\frac{-2 \cos x}{e^{\cos x}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=\log \left[\mathrm{e}^{5 x}\left(\frac{3 x-4}{x+5}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}}\right]$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} y & =\log \left[\mathrm{e}^{5 x}\left(\frac{3 x-4}{x+5}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}}\right] \\ \therefore \quad y & =5 x \log \mathrm{e}+\frac{4}{3} \log (3 x-4)-\frac{4}{3} \log (x+5) \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =5+\frac{4}{3(3 x-4)} \times 3-\frac{4}{3(x+5)} \times 1 \\ & =5+\frac{4}{(3 x-4)}-\frac{4}{3(x+5)} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $f$ be a twice differentiable function such that $\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\mathrm{f}(x), \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\mathrm{g}(x)$ and $\mathrm{h}(x)=[\mathrm{f}(x)]^2+[\mathrm{g}(x)]^2$. If $\mathrm{h}(5)=1$, then $\mathrm{h}(10)$ is __________.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}(x) & =[\mathrm{f}(x)]^2+[\mathrm{g}(x)]^2 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{~h}^{\prime}(x) & =2[\mathrm{f}(x)] \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)+2[\mathrm{~g}(x)] \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(x) \\ & =2\left[-\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)\right] \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)+2\left[\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)\right] \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x) \\ & =0 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \mathrm{h}(x) \text { is a constant function. }\\ &\therefore \quad h(5)=1 \Rightarrow h(10)=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=1$ is equal to
explanation:
To find $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}$ when $y = \sec(\tan^{-1} x)$, let's solve step-by-step:

Expression for $y$:

$ y = \sec(\tan^{-1} x) $

Let $\theta = \tan^{-1} x$ so that $\tan \theta = x$. Therefore:

$ y = \sec \theta $

Right Triangle Approach:

When $\tan \theta = x$, consider a right triangle where the opposite side to angle $\theta$ is $x$ and the adjacent side is 1. The hypotenuse $h$ is determined by the Pythagorean theorem:

$ h = \sqrt{x^2 + 1} $

Expression for $\sec \theta$:

$ \sec \theta = \frac{h}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 1}}{1} = \sqrt{x^2 + 1} $

Therefore, $y = \sqrt{x^2 + 1}$.

Derivative $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}$:

$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} = \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}(\sqrt{x^2 + 1}) $

Using the chain rule:

$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} = \frac{1}{2}(x^2 + 1)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}(x^2 + 1) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x^2 + 1}} \cdot 2x = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 1}} $

Evaluate at $x = 1$:

$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} \bigg|_{x=1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $

The value of $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}$ at $x = 1$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

Option C: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\log _{x^2}\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right)$, then $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)$ at $x=\mathrm{e}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =\log _{x^2}\left(\log _e x\right) \\ & =\frac{\log \left(\log _e x\right)}{\log x^2} \\ & =\frac{\log \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right)}{2 \log x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\log x \cdot \frac{1}{\log _{\mathrm{e}} x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}(\log x)-\log \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right) \cdot \frac{1}{x}}{(\log x)^2}\right] \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\frac{1}{x}-\frac{\log \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right)}{x}}{\left(\log _x\right)^2}\right] \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{e})=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\frac{1}{(-0}}{(1)^2}\right]=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{e}} \end{array}$$

content: If $x=\sec \theta-\cos \theta, y=\sec ^{10} \theta-\cos ^{10} \theta$ and $\left(x^2+4\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=k\left(y^2+4\right)$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & x=\sec \theta-\cos \theta \text { and } y=\sec ^{10} \theta-\cos ^{10} \theta \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=\sec \theta \tan \theta+\sin \theta \text { and } \\ & \frac{d y}{d \theta}=10 \sec ^9 \theta \cdot \sec \theta \tan \theta-10 \cos ^9 \theta \cdot(-\sin \theta) \\ & =10 \sec ^{10} \theta \tan \theta+10 \cos ^9 \theta \sin \theta \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}=\frac{10 \sec ^{10} \theta \tan \theta+10 \cos ^9 \theta \sin \theta}{\sec \theta \tan \theta+\sin \theta} \\ & =\frac{10\left(\sec ^{10} \theta+\cos ^{10} \theta\right)}{\sec \theta+\cos \theta} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2=\frac{10^2\left(\sec ^{10} \theta+\cos ^{10} \theta\right)^2}{(\sec \theta+\cos \theta)^2} \\ & =\frac{100\left[\left(\sec ^{10} \theta-\cos ^{10} \theta\right)^2+4 \sec ^{10} \theta \cos ^{10} \theta\right]}{(\sec \theta-\cos \theta)^2+4 \sec \theta \cos \theta} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=\frac{100\left(y^2+4\right)}{x^2+4} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(x^2+4\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2=100\left(y^2+4\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=100 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \cdot 3^x}{1+9^x}\right)$, then $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ equals
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2.3^x}{1+9^x}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2.3^x}{1+\left(3^x\right)^2}\right)\\ &\text { Put } 3^x=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(3^x\right)\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) & =\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1+\tan ^2 \theta}\right) \\ & =\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta) \\ & =2 \theta \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{f}(x)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(3^x\right) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=2 \cdot \frac{1}{1+\left(3^x\right)^2} \cdot 3^x \log 3 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) & =2 \cdot \frac{1}{1+\left(3^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)^2} \cdot 3^{\frac{1}{2}} \log 3 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{3} \log 3=\sqrt{3} \log \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{F}(x)=\left(\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^2$, where $\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\mathrm{f}(x)$ and $\mathrm{g}(x)=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)$ and given by $\mathrm{F}(5)=5$, then $F(10)$ is equal to
explanation:
Given the problem, it's helpful to first deduce the forms of $ f(x) $ and $ g(x) $.

Differential Equation: The differential equation $ f^{\prime \prime}(x) = -f(x) $ suggests a trigonometric solution, typically of the form:

$$ f(x) = A \cos(x) + B \sin(x) $$

where $ A $ and $ B $ are constants.

Relation of $ g(x) $: Since $ g(x) = f^{\prime}(x) $, we differentiate $ f(x) $:

$$ f^{\prime}(x) = -A \sin(x) + B \cos(x) $$

Therefore, we have:

$$ g(x) = -A \sin(x) + B \cos(x) $$

Expression for $ F(x) $: We need to evaluate $ F(x) $:

$$ F(x) = \left( f\left( \frac{x}{2} \right) \right)^2 + \left( g\left( \frac{x}{2} \right) \right)^2 $$

Evaluate at $ x = 5 $: We know $ F(5) = 5 $:

Substitute $ \frac{x}{2} = \frac{5}{2} $:

$$ F(5) = \left( A \cos\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) + B \sin\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) \right)^2 + \left( -A \sin\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) + B \cos\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) \right)^2 = 5 $$

This implies:

$$ A^2 \left( \cos^2\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) + \sin^2\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) \right) + B^2 \left( \sin^2\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) + \cos^2\left( \frac{5}{2} \right) \right) = 5 $$

Simplifying using $ \cos^2(x) + \sin^2(x) = 1 $:

$$ A^2 + B^2 = 5 $$

Determine $ F(10) $: Now evaluate $ F(10) $:

Substitute $ \frac{x}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5 $:

$$ F(10) = \left( A \cos(5) + B \sin(5) \right)^2 + \left( -A \sin(5) + B \cos(5) \right)^2 $$

As before, we simplifying yields:

$$ A^2 + B^2 = 5 $$

Therefore, $ F(10) = 5 $.

Thus, the value of $ F(10) $ is $\boxed{5}$.

content: The approximate value of $\sqrt[3]{0.026}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{f}(x)=\sqrt[3]{x}=x^{\frac{1}{3}} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{3} x^{-\frac{2}{3}}=\frac{1}{3 x^{\frac{2}{3}}} \end{aligned}$$

Here, $\mathrm{a}=0.027$ and $\mathrm{h}=-0.001$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad f(a) & =f(0.027)=(0.027)^{\frac{1}{3}}=0.3 \text { and } \\ f^{\prime}(a) & =f^{\prime}(0.027) \\ & =\frac{1}{3(0.027)^{\frac{2}{3}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{3(0.09)}=\frac{1}{0.27} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{h}) & \approx \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})+\mathrm{hf}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a}) \\ & \approx 0.3-\frac{0.001}{0.27} \\ & \approx 0.3-0.0037 \\ & \approx 0.2963 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=\left[\mathrm{e}^{4 x}\left(\frac{x-4}{x+3}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}\right]$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$
explanation:
$$y=\mathrm{e}^{4 x}\left(\frac{x-4}{x+3}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}$$

Taking log on both sides,

$$\begin{aligned} & \log y=\log \left[e^{4 x}\left(\frac{x-4}{x+3}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}\right] \\ & \log y=\log e^{4 x}+\log \left(\frac{x-4}{x+3}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}} \\ & \log y=4 x \log e+\frac{3}{4} \log \left(\frac{x-4}{x+3}\right) \\ & \log y=4 x+\frac{3}{4} \log \left(\frac{x-4}{x+3}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.to $x$ on both sides,

$$\begin{aligned} \quad \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =4+\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{\frac{(x-4)}{(x+3)}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(\frac{x-4}{x+3}\right) \\ \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =4+\frac{3}{4}\left(\frac{x+3}{x-4}\right) \times\left(\frac{(x+3)-(x-4)}{(x+3)^2}\right) \\ \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =4+\frac{3}{4}\left(\frac{1}{x-4}\right) \times\left(\frac{x+3-x+4}{(x+3)}\right) \\ \quad \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =4+\frac{3}{4} \times\left(\frac{1}{x-4}\right) \times\left(\frac{7}{x+3}\right) \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =y\left[4+\frac{21}{4(x-4)(x+3)}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=a \sin x+b \cos x \quad$ (where $\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{b}$ are constants), then $y^2+\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2$ is
explanation:
$$y=a \sin x+b \cos x$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{d y}{d x}=a \cos x-b \sin x$$

$\therefore \quad$ Now,

$$y^2+\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} &=(\mathrm{a} \sin x+\mathrm{b} \cos x)^2+(\mathrm{a} \cos x-\mathrm{b} \sin x)^2 \\ &= \mathrm{a}^2 \sin ^2 x+\mathrm{b}^2 \cos ^2 x+2 \mathrm{ab} \sin x \cos x+\mathrm{a}^2 \cos ^2 x \\ & \quad+\mathrm{b}^2 \sin ^2 x-2 \mathrm{ab} \sin x \cdot \cos x \\ &= \mathrm{a}^2\left(\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x\right)+\mathrm{b}^2\left(\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x\right) \\ &= \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad y^2+\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2=\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 \end{aligned}$$

Here, $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ are constants.

$\therefore \quad y^2+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2$ is also a constant.

content: If $y=\sqrt{\frac{1-\sin ^{-1} x}{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}$, then $\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)$ at $x=0$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &y=\sqrt{\frac{1-\sin ^{-1} x}{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-\sin ^{-1} x}{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\frac{1-\sin ^{-1} x}{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(\frac{1-\sin ^{-1} x}{1+\sin ^{-1} x}\right) \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}{2 \sqrt{1-\sin ^{-1} x}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \cdot\frac{\left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(1-\sin ^{-1} x\right)-\left(1-\sin ^{-1} x\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}{2 \sqrt{1-\sin ^{-1} x}} \\ & \cdot \frac{\left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\left(1-\sin ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}}{\left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right)^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \begin{array}{l} =\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}{2 \sqrt{1-\sin ^{-1} x}} \times \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\left[-1-\sin ^{-1} x-1+\sin ^{-1} x\right]}{\left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right)^2} \\ \begin{array}{c} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(-1) \sqrt{1+\sin ^{-1} x}}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)\left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right)^2} \\ \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_\mathrm{at~ x=0} \quad=\frac{-1 \sqrt{1+\sin ^{-1} 0}}{\left(\sqrt{1-\sin ^1 0}\right)\left(\sqrt{1-0^2}\right)\left(1+\sin ^{-1} 0\right)^2} \\ \quad=\frac{-1}{(1)(1)(1+0)} \end{array} \\ \therefore\quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_\mathrm{at~{x=0} }\quad=-1 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x=\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} t}}$$ and $$y=\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^{\cos ^{-1} t}}$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} x y & =\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} t}} \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{e}^{\cos ^{-1} t}} \\ & =\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} t+\cos ^{-1} t}} \\ \therefore \quad x y & =\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}} \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y \cdot 1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\frac{y}{x} \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2} & =-\left(\frac{x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y \cdot 1}{x^2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2} & =-\left(\frac{x\left(-\frac{y}{x}\right)-y}{x^2}\right) \quad \text{... [From (i)]}\\ & =\frac{2 y}{x^2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\sin (\log x)$$ and $$y=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{3-2 x}\right)$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ at $$x=1$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \quad y=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{3-2 x}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{3-2 x}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{3-2 x}\right) \\ &=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{3-2 x}\right) \cdot \frac{(3-2 x) \cdot 2-(2 x+3)(-2)}{(3-2 x)^2} \\ &=\sin \left[\log \left(\frac{2 x+3}{3-2 x}\right)\right] \cdot \frac{12}{(3-2 x)^2} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=1}=\sin (\log 5) \cdot 12=12 \sin (\log 5) \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\mathrm{P}(x)$$ be a polynomial of degree 2, with $$\mathrm{P}(2)=-1, \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(2)=0, \mathrm{P}^{\prime \prime}(2)=2$$, then $$\mathrm{P}(1.001)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{P}(x)=a x^2+b x+c \\ & \Rightarrow P^{\prime}(x)=2 a x+b \\ & \Rightarrow P^{\prime \prime}(x)=2 a \\ & P^{\prime \prime}(2)=2 a \\ & \Rightarrow 2=2 a \\ & \Rightarrow a=1 \\ & P^{\prime}(2)=2 a(2)+b \\ & \Rightarrow 0=4 a+b \\ & \Rightarrow 0=4(1)+b \\ & \Rightarrow b=-4 \\ & P(2)=a(2)^2+b(2)+c \\ & \Rightarrow-1=4 a+2 b+c \\ & \Rightarrow-1=4(1)+2(-4)+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{P}(x)=x^2-4 x+3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(x)=2 x-4 \\ & x=1.001=1+0.001=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{h} \end{aligned}$$

Here, $$\mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~h}=0.001$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a})=\mathrm{P}(1)=1-4+3=0 \\ & \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})=\mathrm{P}^{\prime}(1)=2-4=-2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{P}(1.001)=0+(0.001)(-2)=-0.002$$

content: If $$y=\sqrt{(x-\sin x)+\sqrt{(x-\sin x)+\sqrt{(x-\sin x) \ldots.}}}$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=$$
explanation:
To solve this problem, let's first observe the nested square root structure within the equation $$y=\sqrt{(x-\sin x)+\sqrt{(x-\sin x)+\sqrt{(x-\sin x) \ldots.}}}$$ Since the structure of nested square roots repeats indefinitely, we can rewrite this as:

$$y = \sqrt{(x - \sin x) + y}$$

Now let's square both sides:

$$y^2 = (x - \sin x) + y$$

Isolate $$y$$ on one side:

$$y^2 - y = x - \sin x$$

$$y^2 - y - (x - \sin x) = 0$$

This equation gives us a relationship between $$x$$ and $$y$$ that we can differentiate with respect to $$x$$. We will use implicit differentiation, differentiating both sides of the equation with respect to $$x$$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(y^2 - y - (x - \sin x)) = \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(0)$$

When differentiating the left side, keep in mind that $$y$$ is a function of $$x$$ ($$y = f(x)$$). Applying the chain rule and using the fact that $$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(\sin x) = \cos x$$, we get:

$$2y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} - \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} - (1 - \cos x) = 0$$

Reorganize the terms:

$$2y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} - \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} = 1 - \cos x$$

$$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}(2y - 1) = 1 - \cos x$$

Now, solving for $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}$$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x} = \frac{1 - \cos x}{2y - 1}$$

This matches Option A:

$$\boxed{\frac{1-\cos x}{2 y-1}}$$

content: Let $$f: R \rightarrow R$$ be a function such that $$\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+x^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+x \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)+6, x \in \mathrm{R}$$, then $$\mathrm{f}(2)$$ equals
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+x^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+x \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)+6 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2+2 x \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2) \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 x+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \quad \text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $$x=1$$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(1)=3(1)^2+2(1) f^{\prime}(1)+f^{\prime \prime}(2) \\ & \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(1)+f^{\prime \prime}(2)=-3 \quad \text{..... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $$x=2$$ in (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime \prime}(2)=6(2)+2 f^{\prime}(1) \\ & \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(2)=12+2 f^{\prime}(1) \quad \text{..... (iv)} \end{aligned}$$

From (iii) and (iv), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+12+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=-3 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=-15 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=-5 \\ & \text { From (iii), }-5+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)=-3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)=2 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(2)=2^3+2^2(-5)+2(2)+6 \\ & =8-20+4+6 \\ & =-2 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\text { If } y=\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^2+\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^2, \text { then }\left(1-x^2\right) y_2-x y_1=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^2+\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2 \sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{2 \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^2} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} x\right) \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{1-x^2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}} \cdot(-2 x) \\ & =2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{(-1)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)=\frac{4}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=4 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots \ldots(\mathrm{n} x+1)]^n$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ at $$x=0$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots(\mathrm{n} x+1)]^{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \Rightarrow \log y=\operatorname{nlog}[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots(\mathrm{n} x+1)] \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \log y=\mathrm{n}[\log (x+1)+\log (2 x+1) +\log (3 x+1)+\ldots+\log (n x+1)]$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{n}\left(\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{2 x+1}+\frac{3}{3 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n} x+1}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=0}=\mathrm{n}(1+2+3+\ldots+\mathrm{n}) \quad ...[\text{At} ~x=0,y=1]\\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=0}=\mathrm{n}\left[\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}\right]=\frac{\mathrm{n}^2(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$y=\frac{\sqrt[3]{1+3 x} \sqrt[4]{1+4 x} \sqrt[5]{1+5 x}}{\sqrt[7]{1+7 x} \sqrt[8]{1+8 x}} \text {. Then, } \frac{d y}{d x} \text { at } x=0$$ is
explanation:
$$y=\frac{\sqrt[3]{1+3 x} \sqrt[4]{1+4 x} \sqrt[5]{1+5 x}}{\sqrt[7]{1+7 x} \sqrt[8]{1+8 x}}$$

Taking log on both sides,

$$\begin{aligned} \log y= & \frac{1}{3} \log (1+3 x)+\frac{1}{4} \log (1+4 x) \\ & +\frac{1}{5} \log (1+5 x)-\frac{1}{7} \log (1+7 x)-\frac{1}{8} \log (1+8 x) \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$,

$$\frac{1}{y} \times \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+3 x}+\frac{1}{1+4 x}+\frac{1}{1+5 x}-\frac{1}{1+7 x}-\frac{1}{1+8 x}$$

At $$x=0, y=1$$

$$\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=1+1+1-1-1=1$$

content: Let $$f: R \rightarrow R$$ be a function such that $$f(x)=x^3+x^2 f^{\prime}(1)+x f^{\prime \prime}(2)+f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3), x \in R \text {, }$$ then $$f(2)$$ equals
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { } & f(x)=x^3+x^2 f^{\prime}(1)+x f^{\prime \prime}(2)+f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3) \\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2+2 x f^{\prime}(1)+f^{\prime \prime}(2) \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 x+2 f^{\prime}(1) \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime \prime \prime}(x)=6 \quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{array}$$

Put $$x=1$$, in Eq. (i)

$$f^{\prime}(1)=3+2 f^{\prime}(1)+f^{\prime \prime}(2)$$ .... (iv)

Put $$x=2$$ in Eq. (ii)

$$f^{\prime \prime}(2)=12+2 f^{\prime}(1)$$ ..... (v)

From Eqs. (iv) and (v),

$$\begin{array}{ll} & -3-f^{\prime}(1)=12+2 f^{\prime}(1) \\ \Rightarrow & 3 f^{\prime}(1)=-15 \\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(1)=-5 \\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime \prime}(2)=2 \quad \text{[from Eq. (v)]} \end{array}$$

Put $$x=3$$ in Eq. (iii), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3)=6 \\ & f(x)=x^3-5 x^2+2 x+6 \\ & f(2)=8-20+4+6=-2 \end{array}$$

content: If $$x=\log _e\left(\frac{\cos \frac{y}{2}-\sin \frac{y}{2}}{\cos \frac{y}{2}+\sin \frac{y}{2}}\right), \tan \frac{y}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{1-t}{1+t}}$$ Then, $$\left(y_1\right)_{t=1 / 2}$$ has the value
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} x & =\log _e\left(\frac{\cos \frac{y}{2}-\sin \frac{y}{2}}{\cos \frac{y}{2}+\sin \frac{y}{2}}\right) \\ & =\log _e\left(\frac{1-\tan \frac{y}{2}}{1+\tan \frac{y}{2}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

[On dividing $$\cos y / 2$$ in both numerator and denominator]

$$\begin{aligned} & =\log _e\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{\frac{1-t}{1+t}}}{1+\sqrt{\frac{1-t}{1+t}}}\right)=\log _e\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+t}-\sqrt{1-t}}{\sqrt{1+t}+\sqrt{1-t}}\right) \\ & =\log _e\left(\frac{1+t+1-t-2 \sqrt{1-t^2}}{1+t-1+t}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$x=\log _e\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}{t}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{t}{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}\left[\frac{\frac{t^2}{\sqrt{1-t^2}}-\left(1-\sqrt{1-t^2}\right)}{t^2}\right] \\ & \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{t}{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}\left[\frac{t^2-\sqrt{1-t^2}+1-t^2}{\sqrt{1-t^2} t^2}\right] \\ & \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{1}{t \sqrt{1-t^2}} \quad \text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \because \quad \tan \frac{y}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{1-t}{1+t}} \Rightarrow \frac{y}{2}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-t}{1+t}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} \frac{d y}{d t}=\left(\frac{t+1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+t}}{2 \sqrt{1-t}}\right)\left(\frac{-1-t-1+t}{(1+t)^2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} \frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-t^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} \quad \text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right|_{t=\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} \times \frac{t \sqrt{1-t^2}}{1}=-\frac{1}{2}$$

content: Differentiation of $$\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}-1}{x}\right)$$ w.r.t. $$\cos ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1+x^2}}{2 \sqrt{1+x^2}}}\right)$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{u}=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}-1}{x}\right]$$

and $$\mathrm{v}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1+x^2}}{2 \sqrt{1+x^2}}}\right]$$

Put $$x=\tan \theta$$, then $$\theta=\tan ^{-1} x$$
