$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \mathrm{u} =\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2 \theta}-1}{\tan \theta}\right]=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sec \theta-1}{\tan \theta}\right] \\ = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {{{{1 \over {\cos \theta }} - 1} \over {{{\sin \theta } \over {\cos \theta }}}}} \right] = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {{{1 - \cos \theta } \over {\sin \theta }}} \right] \\ =\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}\right]=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{\theta}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{u}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{2} \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \mathrm{v}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1+\tan ^2 \theta}}{2 \sqrt{1+\tan ^2 \theta}}}\right] \\ & =\cos ^{-1}\left[\sqrt{\frac{1+\sec \theta}{2 \sec \theta}}\right] \\ & =\cos ^{-1}\left[\sqrt{\frac{1+\frac{1}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{2}{\cos \theta}}}\right] \\ & =\cos ^{-1}\left[\sqrt{\frac{1+\cos \theta}{2}}\right] \\ & =\cos ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)}{2}}\right) \\ & =\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{\theta}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{v}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{2} \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ \end{aligned}$$

From (i) and (ii), we get $$\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{v}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dv}}=1$$

content: If $$y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 \sin 2 x}{\cos 2 x-6 \sin ^2 x}\right)$$, then $$\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)$$ at $$x=0$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} y & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 \sin 2 x}{\cos 2 x-6 \sin ^2 x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{4(2 \sin x \cos x)}{\left(\cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x\right)-6 \sin ^2 x}\right] \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{8 \sin x \cos x}{\cos ^2 x-7 \sin ^2 x}\right) \\ & \begin{array}{aligned} =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{8 \tan x}{1-7 \tan ^2 x}\right) \\ =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{7 \tan x+\tan x}{1-7 \tan x \cdot \tan x}\right) \\ =\tan ^{-1}(7 \tan x)+\tan ^{-1}(\tan x) \\ \therefore \quad y=\tan ^{-1}(7 \tan x)+x \\ \therefore \quad\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{1+(7 \tan x)^2} \cdot 7 \sec ^2 x+1 \\ =\frac{7 \sec ^2 x}{1+49 \tan ^2 x}+1 \\ \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=0} \quad \frac{7 \sec ^2 0}{1+49 \tan ^2 0}+1 \\ =\frac{7}{1+0}+1 \\ =8 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\mathrm{f}: \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$$ be a function such that $$\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+x^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+x \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)+6, x \in \mathrm{R}$$, then $$\mathrm{f}(2)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+x^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+x \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)+6 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2+2 x \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2) \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 x+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $$x=1$$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=3(1)^2+2(1) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)=-3 \quad \text{... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $$x=2$$ in (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime \prime}(2)=6(2)+2 f^{\prime}(1) \\ & \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(2)=12+2 f^{\prime}(1) \quad \text{... (iv)} \end{aligned}$$

From (iii) and (iv), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+12+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=-3 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=-15 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=-5 \\ & \text { From (iii), }-5+f^{\prime \prime}(2)=-3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)=2 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(2)=2^3+2^2(-5)+2(2)+6 \\ & =8-20+4+6=-2 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \log x}{1+(\log x)^2}\right)$$, then $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{e})$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \log x}{1+(\log x)^2}\right) \\ \therefore \quad f^{\prime}(x)= & \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{2 \log x}{1+(\log x)^2}\right)^2}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1+(\log x)^2}{\sqrt{1+(\log x)^4+2(\log x)^2-4(\log x)^2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}\left(\frac{2 \log x}{1+(\log x)^2}\right) \\ & =\frac{1+(\log x)^2}{\sqrt{1-2(\log x)^2+(\log x)^4}} \\ & =\frac{1}{1-(\log x)^2} \times \frac{2+2(\log x)^2-4(\log x)^2}{x\left[1+(\log x)^2\right]} \\ = & \frac{1}{\left.1-(\log x)^2\right] \times \frac{2}{x}-(2 \log x)\left(\frac{2 \log x}{x}\right)} \times \frac{2\left[1-(\log x)^2\right]}{x\left[1+(\log x)^2\right]} \\ = & \frac{2}{x\left[1+(\log x)^2\right]} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{e}) & =\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}} \end{aligned}$$

content: For $$x>1$$, if $$(2 x)^{2 y}=4 \mathrm{e}^{2 x-2 y}$$, then $$\left(1+\log _e 2 x\right)^2 \frac{d y}{d x}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$(2 x)^{2 y}=4 \mathrm{e}^{2 x-2 y}$$

Taking '$$\log _e$$' on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 y \log _{\mathrm{e}}(2 x)=\log _{\mathrm{e}} 4+(2 x-2 y) \log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{e} \\ & y \log _{\mathrm{e}}(2 x)=\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2+x-y \quad \text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & {\left[\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x\right] \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{2 y}{2 x}=0+1-\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}} \\ & {\left[1+\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x\right] \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1-\frac{y}{x}} \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & \text { Now, (i) } \Rightarrow y=\frac{\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2+x}{1+\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x} \\ \therefore \quad & (\text { ii }) \Rightarrow\left(1+\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x \log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x-\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2}{x\left(1+\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x\right)} \\ \therefore \quad & \left(1+\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x\right)^2 \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x \log _{\mathrm{e}} 2 x-\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2}{x} \end{array}$$

content: If $$\tan y=\frac{x \sin \alpha}{1-x \cos \alpha}$$ and $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{x^2+2 \mathrm{n} x+1}$$, then $$\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \tan y=\frac{x \sin \alpha}{1-x \cos \alpha} \\ & \therefore \quad y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x \sin \alpha}{1-x \cos \alpha}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{x \sin \alpha}{1-x \cos \alpha}\right)^2} \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{x \sin \alpha}{1-x \cos \alpha}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{\frac{1-2 x \cos \alpha+x^2 \cos ^2 \alpha+x^2 \sin ^2 \alpha}{(1-x \cos \alpha)^2}} \\ & \times \frac{(1-x \cos \alpha) \sin \alpha+(x \sin \alpha) \cos \alpha}{(1-x \cos \alpha)^2} \\ & =\frac{\sin \alpha-x \sin \alpha \cos \alpha+x \sin \alpha \cos \alpha}{1+2(-\cos \alpha) x+x^2} \\ & =\frac{\sin \alpha}{x^2+2(-\cos \alpha)+1} \\ & =\frac{m}{x^2+2 n x+1} \quad \text{... [Given]}\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=-\cos \alpha \text { and } \mathrm{m}=\sin \alpha \\ & \Rightarrow m^2+n^2=1 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The approximate value of $$\sin \left(60^{\circ} 0^{\prime} 10^{\prime \prime}\right)$$ is (given that $$\sqrt{3}=1.732,1^{\circ}=0.0175^{\circ}$$ )
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\sin x$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\cos x$$

Here, $$\mathrm{a}=60^{\circ}$$ and

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}=10^{\prime \prime}=\left(\frac{1}{360}\right)^{\circ} & =\frac{1}{360} \times 0.0175^{\mathrm{c}}=0.000049^{\mathrm{c}} \\ \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})=\sin \left(60^{\circ}\right) & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{1.732}{2}=0.866 \\ \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})=\cos (60) & =\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{h}) & \approx \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})+\mathrm{hf}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a}) \\ \therefore \quad \sin \left(60^{\circ} 0^{\prime} 10^{\prime \prime}\right. & \approx 0.866+0.000049 \times 0.5 \\ & \approx 0.866024 \end{aligned}$$

content: The derivative of $$\mathrm{f}(\tan x)$$ w.r.t. $$\mathrm{g}(\sec x)$$ at $$x=\frac{\pi}{4}$$ where $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=2$$ and $$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\sqrt{2})=4$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{p}=\mathrm{f}(\tan x) \text { and } \mathrm{q}=\mathrm{g}(\sec x) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{d} x}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\tan x) \times \sec ^2 x \text { and } \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{d} x}=\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\sec x) \times \sec x \tan x \\ & \left.\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right|_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \times 2=4 \text {, } \\ & \left.\frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{d} x}\right|_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\sqrt{2}) \times \sqrt{2}=4 \sqrt{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Required Derivative }=\left(\frac{\left.\frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{d} x}\right|_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}}{\left.\frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{d} x}\right|_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}}\right)=\frac{4}{4 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x=-1$$ and $$x=2$$ are extreme points of $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\alpha \log x+\beta x^2+x, \alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are constants, then the value of $$\alpha^2+2 \beta$$ is
explanation:
According to the given condition,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=0 \text { and } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(2)=0 \\ & \mathrm{f}(x)=\alpha \log x+\beta x^2+x \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\alpha}{x}+2 \beta x+1\\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(-1)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha+2 \beta=1 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { and } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(2)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha+8 \beta=-2 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \beta=\frac{-1}{2} \text { and } \alpha=2 \\ \therefore \quad & \alpha^2+2 \beta=4-1=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\text { If } \log (x+y)=2 x y \text {, then } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \text { at } x=0 \text { is }$$
explanation:
$$\log (x+y)=2 x y$$ .... (i)

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{1}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=2 y+2 x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{x+y}+\frac{1}{x+y} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 y+2 x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & \text { At } x=0, \text { (i) } \Rightarrow y=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \text { (ii) }\left.\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=0}=1 \end{array}$$

content: $$y=(1+x)\left(1+x^2\right)\left(1+x^4\right) \ldots \ldots \ldots\left(1+x^{2 n}\right)$$, then the value of $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ at $$x=0$$ is
explanation:
$$y=(1+x)\left(1+x^2\right)\left(1+x^4\right) \ldots\left(1+x^{2 n}\right)$$ .... (i)

Taking '$$\log$$' on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \log y=\log (1+x)+\log \left(1+x^2\right) & +\log \left(1+x^4\right) \\ & +\ldots+\log \left(1+x^{2 n}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{1+x}+\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}+\frac{4 x^3}{1+x^4}+\ldots+\frac{2 \mathrm{n} \times x^{2 \mathrm{n}-1}}{1+x^{2 \mathrm{n}}}$$ ..... (ii)

At $$x=0$$, (i) $$\Rightarrow y=1$$

$$\therefore\quad$$ (ii) $$\left.\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=0}=1+0+0+\ldots+0=1$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)=3^x ; \mathrm{g}(x)=4^x$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)-\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(0)}{1+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0) \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(0)}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3^x \log 3 & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)=\log 3 \\ \mathrm{~g}^{\prime}(x)=4^x \log 4 & \Rightarrow \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(0)=\log 4 \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)-\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(0)}{1+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0) \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(0)} & =\frac{\log 3-\log 4}{1+(\log 3)(\log 4)} \\ & =\frac{\log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)}{1+(\log 3)(\log 4)} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\text { For all real } x \text {, the minimum value of } \frac{1-x+x^2}{1+x+x^2} \text { is }$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{1-x+x^2}{1+x+x^2} \\ \therefore \quad & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\left(1+x+x^2\right)(-1+2 x)-\left(1-x+x^2\right)(1+2 x)}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2} \\ = & \frac{\left(-1+2 x-x+2 x^2-x^2+2 x^3\right)-\left(-1+2 x-x-2 x^2+x^2+2 x^3\right)}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2} \\ = & \frac{-2+2 x^2}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2} \end{aligned}$$

If $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=0$$, then $$\frac{-2+2 x^2}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2}=0 \Rightarrow x^2=1$$ $$\Rightarrow x= \pm 1$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x)$$ at $$x=1$$ is $$\frac{1}{3}$$ and $$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ at $$x=-1$$ is 1.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Minimum value of $$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ is $$\frac{1}{3}$$.

content: The set of all points, where the derivative of the functions $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x}{1+|x|}$$ exists, is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ can be written as

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{x}{1-x}, x \leq 0 \\ \frac{x}{1+x}, x>0 \end{array}\right. \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{(1-x)+x}{(1+x)^2}, & x \leq 0 \\ \frac{(1+x)-x}{(1+x)^2}, & x>0\end{cases} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{(1+x)^2} \forall x \in(-\infty, \infty) \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Derivative of } \mathrm{f}(x) \text { exists } \forall x \in(-\infty, \infty) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots(\mathrm{n} x+1)]^{\frac{3}{2}}$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ at $$x=0$$ is
explanation:
$$y=[(x+1)(2 x+1)(3 x+1) \ldots(\mathrm{n} x+1)]^{\frac{3}{2}}$$

Taking '$$\log$$' on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{r} \log y=\frac{3}{2}[\log (x+1)+\log (2 x+1)+\log (3 x+1) \\ +\ldots+\log (\mathrm{n} x+1)] \end{array}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3}{2}\left[\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{2 x+1}+\frac{3}{3 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n} x+1}\right] \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3 y}{2}\left[\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{2 x+1}+\frac{3}{3 x+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n} x+1}\right] \end{aligned}$$

Now at $$x=0, y=[\underbrace{(1)(1)(1) \ldots(1)}_{n \text { times }}]^{\frac{3}{2}}=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \left.\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=0} & =\frac{3(1)}{2}\left[\frac{1}{0+1}+\frac{2}{0+1}+\frac{3}{0+1}+\ldots+\frac{\mathrm{n}}{0+1}\right] \\ & =\frac{3}{2}(1+2+3+\ldots+\mathrm{n}) \\ & =\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2} \\ & =\frac{3 \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=\log _{\sin x} \tan x$$, then $$\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}$$ has the value
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=\frac{\log \tan x}{\log \sin x} \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(\log \sin x)\left(\frac{1}{\tan x}\right) \cdot \sec ^2 x-(\log \tan x)\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right)(\cos x)}{(\log \sin x)^2} \\ & \text { At } x=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{\log \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{1}\right)(\sqrt{2})^2-(\log 1)\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}{\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]^2} \\ & =\frac{-2 \times \frac{1}{2}(\log 2)-0}{\frac{1}{4}(\log 2)^2} \quad \ldots[\because \log 1=0] \\ & =\frac{-4}{\log 2} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\log (\sin x), 0 < x < \pi$$ and $$\mathrm{g}(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\mathrm{e}^{-x}\right), x \geq 0$$. If $$\alpha$$ is a positive real number such that $$\mathrm{a}=(\mathrm{fog})^{\prime}(\alpha)$$ and $$\mathrm{b}=(\mathrm{fog})(\alpha)$$, then
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\log (\sin x), 0 < x < \pi \text { and } \\ & \mathrm{g}(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\mathrm{e}^{-x}\right), x \geq 0 \\ \therefore \quad & (\mathrm{fog})(x)=\log \left[\sin \left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{e}^{-x}\right)\right]=\log \left(\mathrm{e}^{-x}\right)=-x \\ \therefore \quad & (\mathrm{fog})^{\prime}(x)=-1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{a}=(\text { fog })^{\prime}(\alpha)=-1 \text { and } \mathrm{b}=(\text { fog })(\alpha)=-\alpha \\ & \text { These values satisfy only option (B). } \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Option (B) is correct. } \end{array}$$

content: Derivative of $$\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}-\sqrt{1-x^2}}{\sqrt{1+x^2}+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$$ w.r.t. $$\cos ^{-1} x^2$$ is
explanation:
Let $$y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}-\sqrt{1-x^2}}{\sqrt{1+x^2}+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$$ and $$\mathrm{z}=\cos ^{-1}\left(x^2\right)$$

Put $$x^2=\cos 2 \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}-\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} z \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dz}}=-\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$f$$ be a differentiable function such that $$\mathrm{f}(1)=2$$ and $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\mathrm{f}(x)$$, for all $$x \in \mathrm{R}$$. If $$\mathrm{h}(x)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))$$, then $$\mathrm{h}^{\prime}(1)$$ is equal to
explanation:
Given: $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\mathrm{f}(x)$$ for all $$x \in \mathrm{R}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)}{\mathrm{f}(x)}=1$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \log |\mathrm{f}(x)|=x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^{x+\mathrm{c}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^x \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{c}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^x \cdot \mathrm{c}_1 \quad \ldots \text { (i) }\left[\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{c}_1\right] \end{aligned}$$

As $$\mathrm{f}(1)=2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad c_1 \cdot e=2 \\ & \Rightarrow c_1=\frac{2}{e} \end{aligned}$$

Equation (i) becomes

$$f(x)=\mathrm{e}^x \cdot \frac{2}{\mathrm{e}}$$

Now, $$\mathrm{h}(x)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & h^{\prime}(x)=f^{\prime}(f(x)) \times f^{\prime}(x) \\ \therefore \quad & h^{\prime}(1)=f^{\prime}(f(1)) \times f^{\prime}(1) \\ & \Rightarrow h^{\prime}(1)=f^{\prime}(2) \times f^{\prime}(1) \\ & \Rightarrow h^{\prime}(1)=e^2 \times \frac{2}{e} \times 2 \\ & \Rightarrow h^{\prime}(1)=4 e \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y$$ is a function of $$x$$ and $$\log (x+y)=2 x y$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ at $$x=0$$ is
explanation:
$$\log (x+y)=2 x y$$ ...... (i)

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{1}{x+y}\right)\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=2\left(x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1-2 x y-2 y^2}{2 x^2+2 x y-1} \\ & \text { Putting } x=0 \text { in (i), we get } \\ & y=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=0}=\frac{1-0-2}{0+0-1}=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x=3 \tan \mathrm{t}$$ and $$y=3 \sec \mathrm{t}$$, then the value of $$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}$$ at $$\mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi}{4}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & x=3 \tan \mathrm{t} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \sec ^2 \mathrm{t} \\ & y=3 \sec \mathrm{t} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \sec \mathrm{t} \tan \mathrm{t} \\ & \text { Now, } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}}{\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}}=\frac{3 \operatorname{sect} \tan \mathrm{t}}{3 \sec ^2 \mathrm{t}}=\sin \mathrm{t} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\sin \mathrm{t}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{d} x} \\ & =\cos \mathrm{t} \times \frac{1}{3 \sec ^2 \mathrm{t}} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\cos ^3 \mathrm{t}}{3} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}\right)_{\left(1-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}=\frac{\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}\right)^3}{3}=\frac{1}{6 \sqrt{2}} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{e}}{x^2}\right)}{\log \left(e x^2\right)}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4+2 \log x}{1-8 \log x}\right)$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \quad y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{e}}{x^2}\right)}{\log \left(e x^2\right)}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4+2 \log x}{1-8 \log x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\log e-\log x^2}{\log e+\log x^2}\right)+\tan ^{-1}(4)+\tan ^{-1}(2 \log x) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-2 \log x}{1+2 \log x}\right)+\tan ^{-1}(4)+\tan ^{-1}(2 \log x) \\ & \quad=\tan ^{-1}(1)-\tan ^{-1}(2 \log x)+\tan ^{-1}(4)+\tan ^{-1}(2 \log x) \\ & \therefore \quad y=\tan ^{-1}(1)+\tan ^{-1}(4) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{\sqrt{x^4+\mathrm{a}^4}}\right)$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{\sqrt{x^4+a^4}}\right) \\ & \text { Put } x^2=a^2 \tan \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^2}{a^2}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{a^2}{\sqrt{a^4 \tan ^2 \theta+a^4}}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{a^2}{\sqrt{a^4\left(1+\tan ^2 \theta\right)}}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sec \theta}\right) \\ \Rightarrow y & =\cos ^{-1}(\cos \theta) \\ \Rightarrow & y=\theta \\ \Rightarrow & y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^2}{a^2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{x^2}{a^2}\right)^2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{x^2}{a^2}\right) \\ & =\frac{a^4}{a^4+x^4} \cdot \frac{2 x}{a^2} \\ & =\frac{2 a^2 x}{x^4+a^4} \end{aligned}$$

content: For $$x>1$$, if $$(2 x)^{2 y}=4 \mathrm{e}^{2 x-2 y}$$, then $$(1+\log 2 x)^2 \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$(2 x)^{2 y}=4 \mathrm{e}^{2 x-2 y}$$

Taking log on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 y \log 2 x=\log \left(4 \mathrm{e}^{2 x-2 y}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y \log 2 x=\log 4+\log \mathrm{e}^{2 x-2 y} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y \log 2 x=\log 4+2 x-2 y \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y \log 2 x+2 y=\log 4+2 x \\ & \Rightarrow 2(y \log 2 x+y)=2 \log 2+2 x \\ & \Rightarrow y \log 2 x+y=\log 2+x \\ & \Rightarrow y(1+\log 2 x)=x+\log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{x+\log 2}{1+\log 2 x} \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(1+\log 2 x)(1+0)-(x+\log 2)\left(\frac{1}{2 x}\right) \cdot 2}{(1+\log 2 x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(1+\log 2 x)-\frac{1}{x}(x+\log 2)}{(1+\log 2 x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\log 2 x)^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=1+\log 2 x-1-\frac{\log 2}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\log 2 x)^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 2 x-\frac{\log 2}{x} \\ & =\frac{x \log 2 x-\log 2}{x} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^x, \mathrm{~g}(x)=\sin ^{-1} x$$ and $$\mathrm{h}(x)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{h}^{\prime}(x)}{\mathrm{h}(x)}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}(x) & =\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x)) \\ & =\mathrm{f}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right) \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{h}(x) & =\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}^{\prime}(x) & =\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \end{aligned} \\ & \text { Now, } \frac{\mathrm{h}^{\prime}(x)}{\mathrm{h}(x)}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}}{\mathrm{e}^{\sin ^{-1} x}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y$$ is a function of $$x$$ and $$\log (x+y)=2 x y$$, then the value of $$y^{\prime}(0)$$ is
explanation:
$$\log (x+y)=2 x y$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=2 x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y \\ & \frac{1}{x+y}+\frac{1}{(x+y)} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y \\ & \left(\frac{1}{x+y}-2 x\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 y-\frac{1}{x+y} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(\frac{1}{x+y}-2 x\right)=2 y-\frac{1}{x+y} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\left(2 y-\frac{1}{x+y}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{x+y}-2 x\right)} \\ & \text { For } x=0, \log (y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y=1 \\ & \left.\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{(0,1)}=\frac{\left(2-\frac{1}{0+1}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{0+1}-0\right)}=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x^{\mathrm{k}}+y^{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{k}>0)$$ and $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=0$$, then $$\mathrm{k}$$ has the value
explanation:
$$x^k+y^k=a^k$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \mathrm{kx}^{\mathrm{k}-1}+\mathrm{k} y^{\mathrm{k}-1} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\frac{\mathrm{k} x^{k-1}}{\mathrm{k} y^{k-1}} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^{\mathrm{k}-1} \\ & \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{1-k}=0 \\ & \text { But } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=0 \quad \text{... [Given]} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing above equations, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 1-\mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{3} \\ & 1-\frac{1}{3}=\mathrm{k} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{g}$$ is the inverse of $$\mathrm{f}$$ and $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^3}$$, then $$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(x)$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{g}(x) \text { is inverse of function } \mathrm{f}(x) \\ & \text { i.e., } \mathrm{g}(x)=\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x) \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))=x \\ & \text { differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get } \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{g}(x)) \times \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(x)=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{g}(x))} \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { Now, } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^3} \quad \text{....[Given]}\\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{g}(x))=\frac{1}{1+(\mathrm{g}(x))^3} \quad \ldots \text { (ii) } \\ \therefore \quad & \text { From (ii) and }(\text {ii}), \text { we get } \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(x)=1+(\mathrm{g}(x))^3 \end{array}$$

content: The rate of change of $$\sqrt{x^2+16}$$ with respect to $$\frac{x}{x-1}$$ at $$x=5$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} \quad y=\sqrt{x^2+16} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^2+16}} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+16}} \quad \text{..... (i)}\\ \text { Let } \mathrm{z}=\frac{x}{x-1} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dz}}{\mathrm{d} x}=\frac{(x-1)-x}{(x-1)^2} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dz}}{\mathrm{d} x}=\frac{-1}{(x-1)^2} \quad \text{..... (ii)}\\ \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dz}}\right)_{x=5}=\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+16}}}{\frac{-1}{(x-1)^2}} \\ \quad=\frac{-5}{\sqrt{25+16}} \times 16=\frac{-80}{\sqrt{41}} \end{array}$$

content: If $$x^2+y^2=\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}$$ and $$x^4+y^4=\mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2}$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$x^2+y^2=t+\frac{1}{t}$$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$x^4+y^4+2 x^2 y^2=t^2+\frac{1}{t^2}+2$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad \mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2}+2 x^2 y^2=\mathrm{t}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2}+2 \quad ... [\because x^4+y^4=t^2+\frac{1}{t^2},\text{given}]\\ \\ 2 x^2 y^2=2 \\ x^2 y^2=1 \\ \text { differentiating w.r.t. } x, \text { we get } \\ x^2 2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 x y^2=0 \\ x^2 2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-2 x y^2 \\ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-2 x y^2}{2 x^2 y}=\frac{-y}{x} \end{array}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(1)=1, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=3$$, then the derivative of $$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2$$ at $$x=1$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(f(f(x))) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(x)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)+2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \\ & \left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right|_{x=1}=f^{\prime}(f(f(1))) \cdot f^{\prime}(f(1)) \cdot f^{\prime}(1)+2 f(1) f^{\prime}(1) \\ & =3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3+2 \cdot 1 \cdot 3 \\ & =33 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The derivative of $$\mathrm{f}(\sec x)$$ with respect to $$g(\tan x)$$ at $$x=\frac{\pi}{4}$$, where $$f^{\prime}(\sqrt{2})=4$$ and $$g^{\prime}(1)=2$$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\mathrm{f}(\sec x) \text { and } \mathrm{z}=\mathrm{g}(\tan x) \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\sec x) \cdot \sec x \tan x \\ & \frac{d z}{d x}=g^{\prime}(\tan x) \cdot \sec ^2 x \\ & \text { Now, } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dz}}=\frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\sec x) \sec x \tan x}{\mathrm{~g}^{\prime}(\tan x) \sec ^2 x} \\ & \frac{d y}{d z}=\frac{f^{\prime}(\sec x) \tan x}{g^{\prime}(\tan x) \cdot \sec x} \\ & \left.\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dz}}\right|_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\sec \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \tan \frac{\pi}{4}}{\mathrm{~g}^{\prime}\left(\tan \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \sec \frac{\pi}{4}} \\ & =\frac{f^{\prime}(\sqrt{2}) \cdot(1)}{g^{\prime}(1) \cdot \sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{4 \times 1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \\ & =\sqrt{2} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=\log \sqrt{\frac{1+\sin x}{1-\sin x}}$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ at $$x=\frac{\pi}{3}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=\log \left[\left(\frac{1+\sin x}{1-\sin x}\right)^{1 / 2}\right] \\ & \therefore y=\frac{1}{2}[\log (1+\sin x)-\log (1-\sin x)] \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{1+\sin x}(\cos x)-\frac{1}{1-\sin x}(-\cos x)\right] \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{2}(\cos x)\left[\frac{1}{1+\sin x}+\frac{1}{1-\sin x}\right] \\ & \quad=\frac{\cos x}{\cos ^2 x} \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{\cos ^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\left.\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=\pi / 3}=\frac{1}{\cos \frac{\pi}{3}}=\frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}=2$$

content: If $$y=\sin \left(2 \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}}\right)$$ then $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$y=\sin \left\{2 \cdot \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}}\right\}$$

Putting $$x=\cos 2 \theta$$,

$$\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}}=\ldots=\cot \theta=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore y & =\left\{\sin 2 \cdot \tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right]\right\} \\ & =\sin \left\{2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right\} \\ & =\sin (\pi-2 \theta) \\ & =\sin 2 \theta \\ & =\sqrt{1-\cos ^2 2 \theta} \\ & =\sqrt{1-x^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}\left(1-x^2\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}} \times(-2 x) \\ & =\frac{-x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y^{\frac{1}{m}}+y^{\frac{-1}{m}}=2 x, x \neq 1$$, then $$\left(x^2-1\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\because y^{1 / m}+y^{-1 / m}=2 x, \quad \ldots x \neq 1$$ .... (1)

$$\therefore$$ Squaring both sides,

$$y^{2 / m}+y^{-2 / m}+2=4 x^2$$

$$\therefore$$ adding ($$-4$$) to both sides

$$y^{2 / m}+y^{-2 / m}-2=4 x^2-4$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left(y^{1 / m}-y^{-1 / m}\right)^2=4\left(x^2-1\right) \\ & \therefore y^{1 / m}-y^{-1 / m}=2 \sqrt{x^2-1} \quad \text{..... (2)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(1)+(2) \Rightarrow 2 y^{1 / m}=2 x+2 \sqrt{x^2-1} \\ & \therefore \quad y^{1 / m}=x+\sqrt{x^2-1} \\ & \therefore \quad y=\left(x+\sqrt{x^2-1}\right)^m \\ & \therefore \quad \log y=m \cdot \log \left(x+\sqrt{x^2-1}\right) \\ & \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Differentiating b.s. w.r.t. $$x$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=m \cdot \frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^2-1}} \cdot\left[1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x^2-1}} \cdot 2 x\right] \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{m y}{x+\sqrt{x^2-1}} \times \frac{\sqrt{x^2-1}+x}{\sqrt{x^2-1}} \\ & \therefore \sqrt{x^2-1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=m y \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Squaring b.s. : $$\left(x^2-1\right) \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2=m^2 y^2$$

content: If $$y=1+x e^y$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=1+x e^y \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=0+x e^y \frac{d y}{d x}+e^y \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}\left(x e^y-1\right)=-e^y \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-e^y}{x e^y-1} \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-e^y}{\left(1+x e^y\right)-2}=\frac{-e^y}{y-2}=\frac{e^y}{2-y} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x=e^t(\sin t-\cos t)$$ and $$y=e^t(\sin t+\cos t)$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ at $$t=\frac{\pi}{3}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & x=e^t(\sin t-\cos t) \text { and } y=e^t(\sin t+\cos t) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d x}{d t}=e^t(\sin t-\cos t)+e^t(\cos t+\sin t)=2 e^t \sin t \\ & \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=e^t(\sin t+\cos t)+e^t(\cos t-\sin t)=2 e^t \cos t \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 e^t \cos t}{2 e^t \sin t} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{t=\frac{\pi}{3}}=\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 y=1$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 y=1 \\ & \therefore 2 \sin x \cos x-2 \cos y \sin y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x} \quad=\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \sin y \cos y}=\frac{\sin 2 x}{\sin 2 y} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$ \text { If } u=\cos ^3 x, v=\sin ^3 x \text {, then }\left(\frac{d v}{d u}\right)_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}} \text { is equal to } $$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & u=\cos ^3 x \text { and } v=\sin ^3 x \\ & \therefore \frac{d u}{d x}=3 \cos ^2 x(-\sin x) \quad \text { and } \quad \frac{d v}{d x}=3 \sin ^2 x(\cos x) \\ & \therefore \frac{d v}{d u}=\frac{3 \sin ^2 x \cos x}{-3 \sin x \cos ^2 x}=-\tan x \\ & \therefore\left(\frac{d v}{d u}\right)_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=-\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=\log _{10} x+\log _x 10+\log _x x+\log _{10} 10$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=\frac{\log _e x}{\log _e 10}+\frac{\log _e 10}{\log _e x}+1+1 \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x} & =\left(\frac{1}{\log _e 10}\right)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\left(\log _e 10\right)\left[\frac{-\frac{1}{x}}{\left(\log _e x\right)^2}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{x \log _e 10}-\frac{\log _e 10}{x\left(\log _e x\right)^2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=x \tan y$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=x \tan y \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=x \sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}+\tan y \\ & \therefore\left(x \sec ^2 y-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=-\tan y \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\tan y}{x \sec ^2 y-1}=\frac{-x \tan y}{x^2 \sec ^2 y-x}=\frac{-x \tan y}{x^2\left(1+\tan ^2 y\right)-x}=\frac{-x \tan y}{x^2+x^2 \tan ^2 y-x} \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2-x}=\frac{y}{x-x^2-y^2} \quad \cdots[\because y=x \tan y, \text { given }] \end{aligned}$$

content: The derivative of the function $$\cot ^{-1}\left[(\cos 2 x)^{1 / 2}\right]$$ at $$x=\pi / 6$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\cot ^{-1}\left[(\cos 2 x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\right]=\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos 2 x}) \\ & \therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-1}{1+(\sqrt{\cos 2 x})^2} \times \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{\cos 2 x}) \\ & =\frac{-1}{1+\cos 2 x} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\cos 2 x}} \times(-2 \sin 2 x)=\frac{2 \sin 2 x}{2(1+\cos 2 x) \sqrt{\cos 2 x}} \\ & \therefore\left[f^{\prime}(x)\right]_{x=\frac{\pi}{6}}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}{\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \sqrt{\cos \frac{\pi}{3}}}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: For all real $$x$$, the minimum value of the function $$f(x)=\frac{1-x+x^2}{1+x+x^2}$$ is
explanation:
We have $$f(x)=\frac{1-x+x^2}{1+x+x^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore f^{\prime}(x) & =\frac{\left(1+x+x^2\right)(2 x-1)-\left(1-x+x^2\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{\left(2 x+2 x^2+2 x^3-x-1-x^2\right)-\left(2 x-2 x^2+2 x^3+1-x+x^2\right)}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{\left(x+x^2+2 x^3-1\right)-\left(x-x^2+2 x^3+1\right)}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{2 x^2-2}{\left(1+x+x^2\right)^2} \text { and when } f^{\prime}(x)=0 \text {, we get } \\ 2\left(x^2-1\right) & =0 \quad x= \pm 1 \end{aligned}$$

When $$x=1, f(x)=\frac{1}{3}$$ and when $$x=-1, f(x)=3$$

Hence minimum value of $$f(x)$$ is $$\frac{1}{3}$$.

content: If $$f(x)=\operatorname{cosec}^{-1}\left[\frac{10}{6 \sin \left(2^x\right)-8 \cos \left(2^x\right)}\right]$$, then $$f^{\prime}(x)=$$
explanation:
$$f(x)=\operatorname{cosec}^{-1}\left[\frac{10}{6 \sin \left(2^x\right)-8 \cos \left(2^x\right)}\right]=\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{6 \sin \left(2^x\right)-8 \cos \left(2^x\right)}{10}\right]$$

Here $$(6)^2+(-8)^2=(10)^2$$

$$\therefore$$ Let $$\cos \alpha=\frac{6}{10}$$ and $$\sin \alpha=\frac{8}{10}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left[\sin \left(2^x\right) \cos \alpha-\cos \left(2^x\right) \sin \alpha\right]=\sin ^{-1}\left[\sin \left(2^x-\alpha\right)\right] \\ & \therefore f(x)=2^x-\alpha \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=2^x \log 2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=\log \sqrt{\tan x}$$, then the value of $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ at $$x=\frac{\pi}{4}$$ is
explanation:
To find the value of $$\frac{dy}{dx}$$ at $$x=\frac{\pi}{4}$$, we first need to determine the derivative of the given function $$y = \log \sqrt{\tan x}$$.

Let's rewrite the function for simplicity. Since $$\log \sqrt{\tan x} = \frac{1}{2} \log \tan x$$, we have:

$$y = \frac{1}{2} \log \tan x$$

Now, we differentiate $$y$$ with respect to $$x$$. Using the chain rule, we get:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\tan x} \cdot \sec^2 x$$

Simplify the expression:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{2 \tan x} \cdot \sec^2 x = \frac{\sec^2 x}{2 \tan x}$$

We know that $$\sec^2 x = 1 + \tan^2 x$$. Hence, the expression becomes:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1 + \tan^2 x}{2 \tan x}$$

We need to find this derivative at $$x = \frac{\pi}{4}$$. At this value of $$x$$, $$\tan \frac{\pi}{4} = 1$$. Therefore:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} \bigg|_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}} = \frac{1 + 1^2}{2 \cdot 1} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

Thus, the value of $$\frac{dy}{dx}$$ at $$x=\frac{\pi}{4}$$ is 1.

The correct option is Option A: 1

content: If $$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right)$$ and $$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{b}\left(\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right)$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=$$
explanation:
$$x=a\left(t-\frac{1}{t}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad y=b\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right) \quad \text{.... (1)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{d x}{d t}=a\left(1+\frac{1}{t^2}\right) \text { and } \frac{d y}{d t}=b\left(1-\frac{1}{t^2}\right) \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b\left(1-\frac{1}{t^2}\right)}{a\left(1+\frac{1}{t^2}\right)}=\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)\left(\frac{t^2-1}{t^2+1}\right) \quad \text{... (2)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { Now } \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}^2-1}{\mathrm{t}}\right) \text { and } \quad \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{b}\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}^2+1}{\mathrm{t}}\right) \quad \ldots \text { [From (1)] }$$

$$\therefore\left(t^2-1\right)=\left(\frac{x}{a}\right) t$$ and $$\left(t^2+1\right)=\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)(t)$$

$$\therefore$$ Eq. (2) becomes

$$\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)(t) \times \frac{1}{\left(\frac{y}{b}\right) \times t}=\frac{b^2 x}{a^2 y}$$

content: If $$y=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+\cos x}{1-\cos x}}$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+\cos x}{1-\cos x}} \\ & =\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2 \cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\cot \frac{x}{2}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{x}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{-1}{2} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x^y \cdot y^x=16$$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}$ at $(2,2)$$ is
explanation:
To find $ \frac{d y}{d x} $ given that $x^y \cdot y^x = 16$, we first need to start with the given expression:

$$x^y \cdot y^x = 16$$

Since we seek $ \frac{d y}{d x} $, we'll differentiate both sides of the equation with respect to $x$, employing the product rule $(uv)' = u'v + uv'$ where necessary, and remembering that when differentiating $x$ with respect to $x$, we simply get 1, but differentiating $y$ with respect to $x$ gives us $y'$ or $ \frac{d y}{d x} $. In cases involving variables raised to variables, we apply the logarithmic differentiation method. However, because this equation involves a product where each term has a different base and exponent, we have to treat each part carefully, keeping in mind that the differentiation of $ x^y $ and $ y^x $ with respect to $x$ necessitates implicit differentiation.

Start by applying logarithm on both sides to simplify the differentiation process:

$$ \log(x^y \cdot y^x) = \log(16) $$

Using properties of logarithms, this simplifies to:

$$ y\log(x) + x\log(y) = \log(16) $$

Next, differentiate both sides with respect to $x$:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}[y\log(x) + x\log(y)] = \frac{d}{dx}[\log(16)] $$

Noting that the right side is a constant, its derivative is 0. On the left side, apply the product rule and remember $ \frac{d}{dx} \log(x) = \frac{1}{x} $:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}[y\log(x)] + \frac{d}{dx}[x\log(y)] = 0 $$

It becomes:

$$ y'\log(x) + \frac{y}{x} + \log(y) + xy'\frac{1}{y} = 0 $$

Upon simplification, this yields:

$$ y'\log(x) + y' + \frac{y}{x} + \log(y) = 0 $$

Then, we can solve for $ y' $ or $ \frac{dy}{dx} $:

$$ y'(\log(x) + 1) = -\frac{y}{x} - \log(y) $$

So, $ \frac{dy}{dx} $ is:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-\frac{y}{x} - \log(y)}{\log(x) + 1} $$

To find $ \frac{d y}{d x} $ at $ (x,y) = (2,2) $, substitute $ x = 2 $ and $ y = 2 $ into the derived expression:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} \bigg|_{(2,2)} = \frac{-\frac{2}{2} - \log(2)}{\log(2) + 1} $$

Since $ \log(2) $ is the natural logarithm of $2$, this further simplifies to:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} \bigg|_{(2,2)} = \frac{-1 - \log(2)}{\log(2) + 1} $$

To simplify, note that both the numerator and denominator have $ \log(2) $, so by factoring it out, you can see that the value simplifies neatly:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} \bigg|_{(2,2)} = -1 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A: $$-1$$

content: If $$y^2=a x^2+b x+c$$, where $$a, b, c$$ are constants, then $$y^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$y^2=a x^2+b x+c$$

Differentiatig w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=2 a x+b \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 y \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=2 a \\ & \therefore y \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=a \\ & \therefore y^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=-\left(a x^2+b x+c\right)\left[\left(\frac{2 a x+b}{2}\right)^2-a\right] \quad \text{.....(1)} \end{aligned}$$

R.H.S. of eq. (1) is a function of '$$x$$' only.

The given parametric equations are:

$$x = \frac{1 - t^2}{1 + t^2}$$

and

$$y = \frac{2 a t}{1 + t^2}$$

To find $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$, we need to compute $$\frac{d y}{d t}$$ and $$\frac{d x}{d t}$$ first, and then form their ratio.

First, let's differentiate $$x$$ with respect to $$t$$:

$$\frac{d x}{d t} = \frac{d}{d t} \left( \frac{1 - t^2}{1 + t^2} \right)$$

Using the quotient rule, $$\frac{d}{d t} \left( \frac{u}{v} \right) = \frac{u' v - u v'}{v^2}$$, where $$u = 1 - t^2$$ and $$v = 1 + t^2$$:

$$u' = \frac{d}{d t} (1 - t^2) = -2 t$$

$$v' = \frac{d}{d t} (1 + t^2) = 2 t$$

Therefore,

$$\frac{d x}{d t} = \frac{(-2 t) (1 + t^2) - (1 - t^2) (2 t)}{(1 + t^2)^2}$$

Simplify the numerator:

$$\frac{d x}{d t} = \frac{-2 t - 2 t^3 - 2 t + 2 t^3}{(1 + t^2)^2} = \frac{-4 t}{(1 + t^2)^2}$$

Now, let's differentiate $$y$$ with respect to $$t$$:

$$\frac{d y}{d t} = \frac{d}{d t} \left( \frac{2 a t}{1 + t^2} \right)$$

Using the quotient rule again, where $$u = 2 a t$$ and $$v = 1 + t^2$$:

$$u' = \frac{d}{d t} (2 a t) = 2 a$$

$$v' = \frac{d}{d t} (1 + t^2) = 2 t$$

Therefore,

$$\frac{d y}{d t} = \frac{(2 a) (1 + t^2) - (2 a t) (2 t)}{(1 + t^2)^2}$$

Simplify the numerator:

$$\frac{d y}{d t} = \frac{2 a + 2 a t^2 - 4 a t^2}{(1 + t^2)^2} = \frac{2 a - 2 a t^2}{(1 + t^2)^2} = \frac{2 a (1 - t^2)}{(1 + t^2)^2}$$

Now, we find $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ by dividing $$\frac{d y}{d t}$$ by $$\frac{d x}{d t}$$:

$$\frac{d y}{d x} = \frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}} = \frac{\frac{2 a (1 - t^2)}{(1 + t^2)^2}}{\frac{-4 t}{(1 + t^2)^2}} = \frac{2 a (1 - t^2)}{-4 t} = \frac{a (1 - t^2)}{-2 t} = \frac{a (t^2 - 1)}{2 t}$$

The correct answer is:

Option D

$$\frac{a\left(t^2 - 1\right)}{2 t}$$

content: If y = 2 sin x + 3 cos x and y + A$$\mathrm{\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}$$ = B, then the values of A, B are respectively
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{y}=2 \sin \mathrm{x}+3 \cos \mathrm{x} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 \cos \mathrm{x}-3 \sin \mathrm{x} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}=-2 \sin \mathrm{x}-3 \cos \mathrm{x}=-(2 \sin \mathrm{x}+3 \cos \mathrm{x})=-\mathrm{y} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{y}+\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}=0 \\ & \text { We have } \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{A} \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}=B \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=1, \mathrm{~B}=0\end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {{{a\cos x - b\sin x} \over {b\cos x + a\sin x}}} \right\}$$, then $${{dy} \over {dx}}$$
explanation:
$$y=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{a \cos x-b \sin x}{b \cos x+a \sin x}\right\}$$

Put $$a=r \cos \alpha, b=r \sin \alpha$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore y & =\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\mathrm{r}(\cos \mathrm{x} \cos \alpha-\sin \mathrm{x} \sin \alpha)}{\mathrm{r}(\sin \alpha \cos \mathrm{x}+\cos \alpha \sin \mathrm{x})}\right\} \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos (\mathrm{x}+\alpha)}{\sin (\mathrm{x}+\alpha)}\right]=\tan ^{-1}[\cot (\mathrm{x}+\alpha)] \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left\{\tan \left[\frac{\pi}{2}-(\mathrm{x}+\alpha)\right]\right\}=\frac{\pi}{2}-(\mathrm{x}+\alpha) \\ \therefore \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}} & =0-(1+0)=-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$e^{-y} \cdot y=x$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & e^{-y} \cdot y=x \\ & \therefore \frac{y}{e^y}=x \Rightarrow y=x e^y \quad \text {....(1) and } \quad e^y=\frac{y}{x} \quad \text {....(2)}\\ & \quad \text { Now } y=x e^y \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=x e^y \frac{d y}{d x}+e^y \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}\left(x e^y-1\right)=-e^y \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-e^y}{x e^y-1} \end{aligned}$$

From (1) and (2), we write

$$\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \times \frac{1}{y-1}=\frac{-y}{x(y-1)}=\frac{y}{x(1-y)}$$

content: If $$y=\operatorname{cosec}^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}+1}{\sqrt{x}-1}\right]+\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}-1}{\sqrt{x}+1}\right]$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=\operatorname{cosec}^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}+1}{\sqrt{x}-1}\right)+\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-1}{\sqrt{x}+1}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-1}{\sqrt{x}+1}\right)+\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-1}{\sqrt{x}+1}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=0\end{aligned}$$

content: The derivative of $$(\log x)^x$$ with respect to $$\log x$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \quad=(\log x)^x \\ & \therefore \log u=x \log [\log (x)] \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{x}{\log (x)} \times \frac{1}{x}+\log (\log x)=\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x} \\ & \therefore \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^x\left[\frac{1}{\log x}+\log (\log x)\right] \quad \text{.....(1)}\\ & \text { Let } v=\log x \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{x} \quad \text{.....(2)}\\ & \therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=(\log x)^x(x)\left[\frac{1}{\log x}+\log (\log x)\right]\quad \text{.....(From (1), (2))}\end{aligned}$$

content: $$y=\sqrt{e^{\sqrt{x}}}$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation:
$$y=\sqrt{e^{\sqrt{x}}} \Rightarrow y^2=e^{\sqrt{x}} $$

Takig log on both sides,

$$2 \log y=\sqrt{x} \log e \Rightarrow 2 \log y=\sqrt{x}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\frac{2}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left[\frac{1}{4 \sqrt{x}}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{e^{\sqrt{x}}}}{4 \sqrt{x}}$$

content: If $$y=\sin ^{-1}\left[\cos \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right]+x^x$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ at $$x=1$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \sin ^{-1}\left[\cos \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right]+x^x \\ & =\sin ^{-1}\left[\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right)\right]+x^x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}+x^x \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=0-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{1+x})+\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^x\right) \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \log \mathrm{u}=\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}+\log \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x}}(1+\log \mathrm{x}) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}}}\right]+\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x}}(1+\log \mathrm{x}) \\ & \therefore\left[\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right]_{\mathrm{x}=1}=\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right)+1 \quad=\frac{-1}{4}+1=\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x=a(t+\sin t), y=a(1-\cos t)$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & x=a(t+\sin t) \quad \text { and } y=a(1-\cos t) \\ & \therefore \frac{d x}{d t}=a(1+\cos t) \quad \text { and } \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=a(\sin t) \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)}{\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)}=\frac{a \sin t}{a(1+\cos t)}=\frac{2 \sin \frac{t}{2} \cos \frac{t}{2}}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{t}{2}}=\tan \frac{t}{2}\end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y=\log \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\sin ^{-1}(\cos x)$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation:
Given the equation :

$ y = \log \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) + \sin^{-1}(\cos x) $

The goal is to find $ \frac{dy}{dx} $.

Step 1 : Convert $ \sin^{-1}(\cos x) $ using the complementary angle identity

From trigonometric identities, we know :

$ \sin^{-1}(\cos x) = \frac{\pi}{2} - x $

This is because $ \sin(\frac{\pi}{2} - x) = \cos(x) $.

So the equation becomes :

$ y = \log \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) + \frac{\pi}{2} - x $

Step 2 : Differentiate term by term

For the term $ \log \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) $ :

Using the chain rule and properties of logarithmic differentiation :

$ \frac{d}{dx} \log \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)} \times \sec^2 \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{2} $

Where :

  • $ \frac{1}{\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)} $ is the derivative of the logarithmic function.
  • $ \sec^2 \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) $ is the derivative of $ \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) $.
  • $ \frac{1}{2} $ is the derivative of $ \frac{x}{2} $.

For the term $ \frac{\pi}{2} - x $ :

The derivative is simply -1, since the derivative of a constant is 0 and the derivative of x is 1.

Combining the two terms, we get :

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)} \times \sec^2 \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{2} - 1 $

Step 3 : Simplify

$ \frac{1}{\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)} $ can be written as $ \frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}}{\sin \frac{x}{2}} $.

And $ \sec^2 \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) $ is $ \frac{1}{\cos^2 \frac{x}{2}} $.

Multiplying these terms :

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}}{\sin \frac{x}{2} \cos^2 \frac{x}{2}} \times \frac{1}{2} - 1 $

Step 4 : Use trigonometric identities to further simplify

Using the identity $ \sin 2\theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta $ :

$ \sin x = 2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2} $

The expression can then be simplified as :

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}} - 1 $

Substitute the value from the double angle identity :

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{\sin x} - 1 $

Which is :

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \operatorname{cosec} x - 1 $

Thus, the solution for $ \frac{dy}{dx} $ is $ \operatorname{cosec} x - 1 $.

$$h(x) =\sqrt{4 f(x)+3 g(x)}$$ ..... (1)

$$\therefore h(1) =\sqrt{4(4)+3(3)}=5$$ .... (2)

Squaring (1), we get

$$[\mathrm{h}(\mathrm{x})]^2=4 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})+3 \mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{x})$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$\mathrm{x}$$, we get $$2 \mathrm{~h}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{h}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=4 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})+3 \mathrm{~g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$$

At $$x=1$$, we get

$$2(5) \mathrm{h}^{\prime}(1)=4(3)+3(4)=24$$ ... [From (2) and data given]

content: If $$x=a \cos \theta, y=b \sin \theta$$, then $$\left[\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right]_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & x=a \cos \theta, y=b \sin \theta \\ & \therefore \frac{d x}{d \theta}=-a \sin \theta, \frac{d y}{d \theta}=b \cos \theta \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b \cos \theta}{-a \sin \theta}=\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right) \cot \theta \\ & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x} \\ & =\frac{d}{d \theta}\left[\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right) \cot \theta\right] \times \frac{1}{\left(\frac{d x}{d \theta}\right)} \\ & =\frac{\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right)\left(-\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta\right)}{-a \sin \theta}=\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right) \times \frac{1}{a} \times \frac{1}{\sin ^3 \theta} \\ & \therefore\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\left(\frac{-b}{a^2}\right)\left[\frac{1}{\left(\sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)^3}\right]=\left(\frac{-b}{a^2}\right)(\sqrt{2})^3=-2 \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{b}{a^2}\right) \\ &\end{aligned}$$

content: If $x=a \sin t-b \cos t, y=a \cos t+b \sin t$, then $y^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+x^2+y^2=$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x=a \sin t-b \cos t \quad\text{... (i)}\\ & y=a \cos t+b \sin t\quad\text{... (ii)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Squaring and adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$x^2+y^2=a^2+b^2$$

On differentiating w.r.t. ' $x$ ', we get

$$\begin{aligned} 2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x} & =0 \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{-x}{y} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Again differentiating w.r.t. ' } x \text { ', we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=-\left(\frac{y-x d y / d x}{y^2}\right) \quad\left[\because \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x}{y}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=-\left(y+\frac{x}{y}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+x^2+y^2=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}{2}\right]$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}=$
explanation:
We have, $y=\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}{2}\right]$

Put, $x=\cos 2 \theta$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad y & =\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{2}\right] \\ y & =\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ y & =\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right)\right) \\ y & =\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta \\ y & =\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x^2 y^2=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x^2+y^2}+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$ then $\frac{d y}{d x}=$

content: If $$f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{2}}\right)$$, then $$f^{\prime}(x)=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \qquad f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{2}}\right) \\ & \text { Put, } x=\cos 2 \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x \\ & \therefore \quad f(x)=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1-\cos 2 \theta}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f(x)=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2}}=\sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f(x)=\theta=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x}}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y}}=0, x \neq y$$, then $$(1+x)^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & & \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x}}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y}} & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x} & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x \sqrt{1+y} & =-y \sqrt{1+x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad x^2(1+y)=y^2(1+x) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2+x^2 y-y^2-x y^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(x^2-y^2\right)+x^2 y-x y^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+y)(x-y)+x y(x-y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(x-y)(x+y+x y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y(1+x)=-x \quad[\because x-y \neq 0] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=\frac{-x}{1+x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left[\frac{(1+x)(1)-(x)}{(1+x)^2}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(1+x)^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=-1 \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\sqrt{\frac{x}{y}}+\sqrt{\frac{y}{x}}=4$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=$$
explanation:
We have,

$$\sqrt{\frac{x}{y}}+\sqrt{\frac{y}{x}}=4$$

Squaring both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x}{y}+\frac{y}{x}+2=16 \\ & x^2+y^2=14 x y \end{aligned}$$

On differentiating w.r.t. '$$x$$', we get

$$\begin{aligned} 2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x} & =14\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}+y\right) \\ x+y \frac{d y}{d x} & =7 x \frac{d y}{d x}+7 y \\ \frac{d y}{d x}(y-7 x) & =7 y-x \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{7 y-x}{y-7 x} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$f(x)=\log (\sec x+\tan x)$$, then $$f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=$$
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =\log (\sec x+\tan x) \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =\frac{1}{\sec x+\tan x}\left(\sec x \tan x+\sec ^2 x\right) \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =\frac{\sec x(\sec x+\tan x)}{\sec x+\tan x} \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =\sec x \\ f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) & =\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=\log \left[\frac{x+\sqrt{x^2+25}}{\sqrt{x^2+25}-x}\right]$ then $\frac{d y}{d x}=\ldots \ldots$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, } y=\log \left[\frac{x+\sqrt{x^2+25}}{\sqrt{x^2+25}-x}\right]\\ &y=\log \left[\frac{\left(x+\sqrt{\left.x^2+25\right)^2}\right.}{x^2+25-x^2}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad y=\log \left[\frac{\left(x+\sqrt{x^2+25}\right)^2}{25}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow y=2 \log \left(x+\sqrt{x^2+25}\right)-\log 25 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { On differentiating both sides w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{2}{x+\sqrt{x^2+25}}\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x^2+25}}(2 x)\right) \\ & =\frac{2}{x+\sqrt{x^2+25}}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x^2+25}+x}{\sqrt{x^2+25}}\right) \\ & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{x^2+25}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\cos 3 x}{\sin 3 x}\right)$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}=$ .......
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\cos 3 x}{\sin 3 x}\right) \\ \Rightarrow\quad & y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sin ^2\left(\frac{3 x}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \frac{3 x}{2} \cos \frac{3 x}{2}}\right) \\ \Rightarrow\quad & y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \left(\frac{3 x}{2}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{3 x}{2}\right)}\right) \\ \Rightarrow\quad & y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \frac{3 x}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow\quad & y=\frac{3 x}{2} \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x=\sin \theta, y=\sin ^3 \theta$ then $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$ is ............
explanation:
We have, $x=\sin \theta, y=\sin ^3 \theta$ $d y$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 \sin ^2 \theta(\cos \theta)}{\cos \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=3 \sin ^2 \theta \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=3(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta) \frac{d \theta}{d x}$ $=3(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta) \frac{1}{\cos \theta}=6 \sin \theta$

at $ \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=6 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=6 \times 1=6$

content: If $x^y=e^{x-y}$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}$ at $x=1$ is ...........
explanation:
Given have, $x^y=e^{x-y}$

taking logon both sides, we get $y \log x=(x-y) \log e=(x-y)\quad\text{.... (i)}$

when $x=1$, then $y(\log 1)=(1-y)$ $\Rightarrow y=1$

On differentiating both sides,

$$y\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{d y}{d x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}(\log x+1)=1-\frac{y}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}(\log x+1)=\frac{x-y}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(x-y)}{x(\log x+1)} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { when } x=1 \text {, then }\\ &\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{1-1}{1(\log 1+1)}=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: Derivative of $\log _{e^2}(\log x)$ with respect to $x$ is
explanation:

content: If $x=\sqrt{a^{\sin ^{-1} t}}, y=\sqrt{a^{\cos ^{-1} t}}$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}=\ldots .$.
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{rlrl} & x =\sqrt{a^{\sin ^{-1} t}}, y=\sqrt{a^{\cos ^{-1} t}} \\ & \therefore x y =\sqrt{a^{\sin ^{-1} t} \cdot a^{\cos ^{-1} t}}=\sqrt{a^{\sin ^{-1} t+\cos ^{-1} t}} \\ &\Rightarrow x y=\sqrt{a^{\pi / 2}} \quad\left(\because \sin ^{-1} t+\cos ^{-1} t=\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \end{array}\\ &\text { On differentiating both sides w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0\\ &\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-y}{x} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x=\log \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}>0$ and $y=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Given, } x=\log \mathrm{t}, y=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \\ & x=\log \mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^x=\mathrm{t} \\ \therefore \quad & y=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^x} \\ & \Rightarrow y=\mathrm{e}^{-x} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\mathrm{e}^{-x} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\mathrm{e}^{-x} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=y \text { or } \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \end{array} $$
